stem: During fluoroscopy, the primary source of radiation exposure to the operator is:
options: Direct beam leakage from the tube housing
Characteristic radiation from the image intensifier
Remnant radiation passing through the patient
rationale: Scatter radiation from the patient is the dominant source of occupational exposure during fluoroscopy. The operator should maximize distance, use shielding (lead apron, thyroid shield, lead glasses), and minimize fluoroscopy time.

stem: The image intensifier in fluoroscopy converts:
options: X-ray photons to a visible light image through a phosphor input screen, photocathode, electron optics, and output phosphor
rationale: Image intensifier: x-rays → light at input phosphor (CsI) → electrons at photocathode → accelerated/focused electrons → bright image at output phosphor. Brightness gain = minification gain × flux gain.

stem: Automatic brightness control (ABC) in fluoroscopy adjusts:
options: kVp, mA, or pulse width to maintain constant image brightness as patient thickness varies
rationale: ABC maintains consistent brightness by adjusting kVp and/or mA. As the beam passes through thicker anatomy, ABC increases technical factors. This prevents under/overexposure during real-time imaging.

stem: Pulsed fluoroscopy reduces patient dose by:
options: Using intermittent x-ray pulses instead of continuous radiation, with the pulse rate controlling temporal resolution
rationale: Pulsed fluoroscopy: x-rays are emitted in short bursts (e.g., 15, 7.5, or 3 pulses/second). Lower pulse rates = lower dose. Trade-off: reduced temporal resolution at very low rates.

stem: The maximum tabletop exposure rate for standard fluoroscopy is limited to:
rationale: FDA limits: standard fluoroscopy ≤ 88 mGy/min (10 R/min), high-level fluoroscopy ≤ 176 mGy/min (20 R/min). An audible alarm sounds after 5 minutes of continuous fluoroscopy.

stem: During a barium enema, the patient is placed in various positions to:
options: Coat the mucosal lining of the colon and demonstrate all segments including flexures
rationale: Position changes during BE: prone, supine, obliques, and lateral decubitus coat the entire colonic mucosa. Double contrast (barium + air) demonstrates polyps, diverticula, and mucosal lesions.

stem: Last image hold (LIH) in fluoroscopy:
options: Displays the last fluoroscopic frame without additional radiation exposure, reducing dose during positioning review
Is used only in cardiac catheterization
rationale: LIH freezes the last fluoroscopic frame on the monitor. Allows review of positioning/anatomy without continued irradiation. Significantly reduces fluoroscopy time and patient dose.

stem: Magnification mode in fluoroscopy:
options: Uses a smaller portion of the input phosphor to magnify the image, but increases patient dose
Uses a larger input phosphor area
Has no effect on dose or image quality
rationale: Mag mode: electron beam focused on smaller area of output phosphor → magnified image with improved spatial resolution. ABC compensates by increasing mA/kVp → higher patient dose.

stem: Flat panel detectors in modern fluoroscopy systems offer advantages over image intensifiers including:
options: No vignetting, no pincushion distortion, wider dynamic range, and more compact design
Lower image quality in all situations
rationale: Flat panel detectors: uniform image across entire field (no vignetting), no geometric distortion, higher DQE, compact/lightweight. Replacing image intensifiers in modern equipment.

stem: During ERCP (endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography), the technologist's primary radiation safety concern is:
options: Prolonged fluoroscopy time resulting in high cumulative dose to both patient and staff
rationale: ERCP involves extended fluoroscopy time. Staff dose management: maximize distance, use ceiling-mounted lead shields, wear protective equipment, use pulsed/low-dose fluoroscopy, track cumulative dose.

stem: The video signal in fluoroscopy is measured in:
options: Lines of resolution or TV lines, which determine spatial resolution of the displayed image
rationale: Fluoroscopic resolution: measured in TV lines or line pairs per mm. Typical: 1-2 lp/mm for image intensifier systems, up to 3+ lp/mm for flat panel detectors. Higher = better detail.

stem: Dose-area product (DAP) in fluoroscopy measures:
options: The total radiation exposure including both dose and irradiated area, serving as a comprehensive dose metric
rationale: DAP (Gy·cm²): integrates dose over the entire irradiated area. Independent of distance. Provides comprehensive patient dose assessment. Used for dose tracking and reference levels.

stem: A voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG) is performed to evaluate:
options: Vesicoureteral reflux and urethral anatomy during bladder filling and voiding under fluoroscopy
rationale: VCUG: contrast instilled into bladder via catheter. Fluoroscopy during filling and voiding demonstrates reflux (graded I-V), urethral strictures, posterior urethral valves, and bladder anatomy.

stem: During an arthrogram, contrast is injected:
options: Directly into a joint space under fluoroscopic guidance to evaluate internal joint structures
rationale: Arthrography: fluoroscopic-guided injection of contrast (iodinated, air, or both) into joint. Evaluates menisci, ligaments, cartilage, and joint capsule integrity. Often followed by CT or MRI.

stem: The sentinel event threshold for fluoroscopy skin dose is:
options: 15 Gy cumulative dose to a single skin area, which may cause deterministic skin injury
rationale: Skin dose thresholds: 2 Gy (transient erythema), 5 Gy (temporary epilation), 15 Gy (delayed skin necrosis possible). Joint Commission sentinel event at 15 Gy. Tracking cumulative dose is essential.

stem: Spot films taken during fluoroscopy differ from standard radiographs in that:
options: They are acquired at higher mA during brief exposures while the image intensifier is in position
They provide lower image quality than any radiograph
rationale: Spot films: higher mA exposures (vs. low-mA continuous fluoroscopy) for diagnostic-quality static images of anatomy during a fluoroscopic procedure. Can be film-based or digital.

stem: Minification gain in an image intensifier is calculated as:
options: (Input phosphor diameter / output phosphor diameter)² — it contributes to overall brightness gain
rationale: Minification gain = (d_input/d_output)². For a 23 cm input and 2.5 cm output: (23/2.5)² = 84.6. Larger input phosphor = greater minification gain. Total brightness gain = minification × flux gain.

stem: Vignetting in an image intensifier causes:
options: Loss of brightness at the periphery of the image compared to the center
Loss of brightness at the center only
rationale: Vignetting: peripheral brightness loss due to electron optics. Electrons from the periphery of the input phosphor travel longer paths and experience more divergence. Not present in flat panel detectors.

stem: S-distortion in fluoroscopic images is caused by:
options: External magnetic fields affecting the electron beam trajectory within the image intensifier
rationale: S-distortion: magnetic fields (Earth's field, nearby equipment) deflect the electron beam in the II tube, causing S-shaped image distortion. Mu-metal shielding minimizes this artifact.

stem: Temporal filtering (recursive filtering) in digital fluoroscopy:
options: Averages multiple frames to reduce quantum noise, but may cause image lag with moving structures
Eliminates all image noise without side effects
rationale: Recursive filtering: weighted averaging of current and previous frames → reduced noise. Trade-off: temporal blur/lag with fast-moving objects (e.g., cardiac, GI peristalsis). Adjustable weighting factor.

stem: The standard mammographic projections include:
options: Craniocaudal (CC) and mediolateral oblique (MLO) views of each breast
rationale: Standard screening mammography: CC (top-to-bottom compression) and MLO (angled compression showing maximum breast tissue including axillary tail). Both breasts = 4 images total.

stem: The target/filter combination used in mammography differs from general radiography because:
options: Molybdenum or rhodium targets with matching filters produce the low-energy x-rays needed for soft tissue contrast
Tungsten targets with heavy filtration are standard
rationale: Mammography uses Mo/Mo, Mo/Rh, or Rh/Rh target/filter combinations. K-edge filtering produces a quasi-monochromatic beam (17-20 keV) optimized for soft tissue contrast differentiation.

stem: Breast compression during mammography is essential because it:
options: Reduces breast thickness, decreases scatter, lowers dose, improves contrast, and reduces geometric unsharpness
Is optional and only for patient comfort
rationale: Compression: reduces thickness (less scatter, lower dose), spreads tissue (reduces overlap), brings structures closer to receptor (less magnification), and immobilizes the breast (reduces motion blur).

stem: The kVp range typically used in mammography is:
options: 22-32 kVp, significantly lower than general radiography to maximize soft tissue contrast
rationale: Mammography: 22-32 kVp produces low-energy beam optimized for photoelectric interactions in soft tissue. Higher kVp = more Compton scatter = less contrast. AEC selects optimal kVp based on breast thickness/density.

stem: The BI-RADS classification system is used to:
options: Standardize mammographic reporting and provide assessment categories (0-6) that guide clinical management
rationale: BI-RADS (Breast Imaging Reporting and Data System): 0=incomplete, 1=negative, 2=benign, 3=probably benign, 4=suspicious, 5=highly suggestive of malignancy, 6=known malignancy. Standardizes communication.

stem: Mammography quality control testing by the technologist includes daily:
options: Phantom image evaluation to verify image quality meets ACR standards
rationale: Daily QC: mammography phantom (ACR phantom) — evaluate for fibers, specks, masses. Must visualize minimum: 4 fibers, 3 speck groups, 3 masses. Also check optical density and contrast.

stem: Digital breast tomosynthesis (DBT) acquires:
options: Multiple low-dose projection images at different angles to create reconstructed slices through the breast, reducing tissue overlap
rationale: DBT (3D mammography): tube arc of 15-50° acquires 9-25 projections. Reconstruction creates 1mm slices. Reduces tissue overlap, improves cancer detection, decreases recall rates.

stem: On the MLO view, the pectoralis muscle should extend to:
options: At least the level of the posterior nipple line (PNL) to confirm adequate posterior tissue is included
Only the top of the image
rationale: MLO quality criteria: pectoralis muscle visible to at least PNL level, inframammary fold included, nipple in profile, no skin folds. Angle typically 30-60° depending on body habitus.

stem: Microcalcifications on mammography are significant because:
options: Clustered microcalcifications may indicate ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) or early invasive cancer
They are artifacts from the detector
rationale: Microcalcifications: tiny calcium deposits. Clustered, pleomorphic, or linear/branching patterns are suspicious for DCIS. Benign calcifications: round, scattered, eggshell, popcorn-like, or vascular.

stem: The mean glandular dose (MGD) for a standard screening mammography should not exceed:
options: 3 mGy (300 mrad) per view for a standard 4.2 cm compressed breast under MQSA regulations
rationale: MQSA dose limit: 3 mGy per view for standard breast. Average clinical MGD: 1-2 mGy per view. Total screening exam (4 views): ~4-8 mGy. DBT adds ~20% more dose per view.

stem: Implant displaced (ID/Eklund) views are performed to:
options: Push breast implants posteriorly and pull native breast tissue forward for better visualization
rationale: Eklund technique: implant pushed back against chest wall, native tissue pulled forward and compressed. Performed in addition to (not replacing) standard CC and MLO views. Improves tissue visualization.

stem: Spot compression mammography is used to:
options: Apply focused compression to a specific area of concern to spread tissue and improve visualization of a lesion
Image the entire breast with less compression
rationale: Spot compression: small compression paddle applied to the area of interest. Displaces overlapping tissue, reduces scatter, improves contrast. Used for evaluation of masses, asymmetries, and architectural distortion.

stem: The MQSA (Mammography Quality Standards Act) requires:
options: FDA-certified facilities, ACR-accredited equipment, qualified personnel, annual inspections, and patient notification of results
Only that technologists wear lead aprons
rationale: MQSA (1992/amended 2004): all mammography facilities must be FDA-certified, use accredited equipment, employ qualified interpreting physicians and technologists, maintain QC programs, and provide results to patients.

stem: Dense breast tissue on mammography:
options: Appears white and can mask cancers, reducing mammographic sensitivity and potentially requiring supplemental screening
rationale: Dense tissue: fibroglandular tissue appears white (radiopaque), can obscure masses (also white). Dense Breast Notification laws in many states. Supplemental screening: ultrasound, MRI, or contrast-enhanced mammography.

stem: The exaggerated CC (XCCL) view is performed to:
options: Visualize the far lateral breast tissue not seen on the standard CC view
rationale: XCCL: patient rotated to include lateral tissue. Tube angled laterally. Demonstrates lesions in the lateral aspect/axillary tail not included on standard CC. Complemented by MLO.

stem: Contrast-enhanced spectral mammography (CESM) involves:
options: IV iodinated contrast injection followed by dual-energy mammographic acquisition to demonstrate enhancing lesions
rationale: CESM: IV iodinated contrast → low-energy + high-energy mammographic pairs → subtraction image highlights enhancement. Detects lesions occult on standard mammography. Alternative to breast MRI.

stem: Wire localization in mammography is performed to:
options: Guide a surgeon to a non-palpable lesion by placing a hookwire under mammographic or stereotactic guidance before excision
rationale: Wire localization: needle with hookwire placed under mammographic guidance at the site of a suspicious non-palpable lesion. Confirms placement with orthogonal views. Patient goes to surgery for excision.

stem: Stereotactic breast biopsy uses:
options: Two angled mammographic images to triangulate the 3D position of a lesion for precise needle placement
rationale: Stereotactic biopsy: images at +15° and -15° angles. Computer calculates x, y, z coordinates of the lesion. Vacuum-assisted or core needle biopsy performed. Used for calcifications and masses.

stem: The automatic exposure control (AEC) sensor in mammography is positioned:
options: Under the breast at the chest wall to ensure adequate exposure to the densest part of the breast
rationale: Mammography AEC: sensor positioned under the densest breast tissue (chest wall region). Ensures adequate penetration of dense tissue. Some systems allow sensor repositioning for different breast compositions.

stem: Galactography (ductography) is performed by:
options: Cannulating and injecting contrast into a discharging breast duct under mammographic guidance to identify intraductal pathology
rationale: Galactography: identifies cause of pathologic nipple discharge. Dilator/cannula inserted into discharging duct orifice, contrast injected, mammographic images taken to show filling defects or duct abnormalities.

stem: The rotating anode in an x-ray tube serves to:
options: Distribute heat over a larger target area, allowing higher tube loading and shorter exposure times
rationale: Rotating anode: target area = focal track circumference × focal spot width. Rotation spreads heat. Allows higher mA and shorter exposures. Most diagnostic tubes use rotating anodes (3,000-10,000 RPM).

stem: The line-focus principle in x-ray tube design:
options: Uses an angled anode face to create an effective focal spot smaller than the actual focal spot, improving resolution while maintaining heat capacity
Eliminates the need for an anode
rationale: Line-focus principle: anode angle (typically 7-17°) → effective focal spot (as seen from below) is smaller than actual focal spot. Smaller effective FS = better resolution. Larger actual FS = better heat dissipation.

stem: A high-frequency x-ray generator produces:
options: A nearly constant potential waveform with very low ripple, resulting in higher beam quality and efficiency compared to single-phase
rationale: High-frequency generators: <1% ripple (nearly constant DC). Higher average photon energy, more efficient x-ray production, shorter exposure times, less patient dose compared to single-phase (100% ripple).

stem: Single-phase generators produce x-rays with:
options: 100% voltage ripple, meaning the voltage drops to zero twice per cycle, resulting in lower average beam energy
rationale: Single-phase: 100% ripple (1-pulse) or 100% ripple (2-pulse). Voltage drops to zero → low-energy photons produced near zero crossings → lower average energy, more skin dose, longer exposure times.

stem: Three-phase 12-pulse generators have a voltage ripple of approximately:
options: 4%, providing more consistent x-ray output than single-phase
rationale: 3-phase 6-pulse: ~13% ripple. 3-phase 12-pulse: ~4% ripple. High-frequency: <1% ripple. Lower ripple = higher average kV = higher average photon energy = more efficient = less patient dose.

stem: Tube rating charts are used to determine:
options: Whether a specific combination of kVp, mA, and exposure time is within the safe operating limits of the x-ray tube
rationale: Tube rating charts: plot mA vs. time for specific kVp values. Points below the curve are safe; above the curve risk tube damage. Must check before making unusual exposures (high mA, long time, high kVp).

stem: Anode heat storage capacity is measured in:
options: Heat units (HU) or joules, representing the maximum thermal energy the anode can store before requiring cooling
rationale: Heat units: HU = kVp × mA × time × generator factor (1.0 for single-phase, 1.35 for 3-phase/high-freq). Anode heat capacity: typically 300,000-1,000,000 HU. Cooling rate: ~100,000 HU/min.

stem: Collimation devices in radiography:
options: Restrict the x-ray beam to the area of clinical interest, reducing patient dose and scatter radiation
Increase the beam size for better coverage
rationale: Collimators: adjustable lead shutters limit beam to region of interest. Reduces irradiated volume → less patient dose + less scatter → better contrast. PBL (positive beam limitation) automatically matches field to cassette size.

stem: The filament in an x-ray tube functions to:
options: Produce electrons through thermionic emission when heated by a low-voltage current
rationale: Cathode filament: tungsten coil heated to ~2200°C by filament current. Thermionic emission releases electrons. Focusing cup directs the electron cloud. Two filaments (large/small) for different focal spot sizes.

stem: Quality control testing for radiographic equipment includes:
options: kVp accuracy, mAs linearity, timer accuracy, half-value layer measurement, collimation alignment, and AEC performance
Only visual inspection of the tube
rationale: QC tests: kVp accuracy (±5%), mAs linearity (coefficient of variation <0.05), timer accuracy (±5%), HVL (adequate filtration), collimation (beam/light field within ±2%), focal spot size, AEC reproducibility.

stem: A grid is characterized by its grid ratio, which is:
options: The ratio of lead strip height to the distance between lead strips (interspace width)
The total number of lead strips
rationale: Grid ratio = h/D (height of lead strips / width of interspace). Common ratios: 5:1 to 16:1. Higher ratio = better scatter cleanup but requires more precise alignment and higher mAs.

stem: Grid cutoff occurs when:
options: The x-ray beam is not properly aligned with a focused grid, causing absorption of primary radiation and image underexposure
The grid ratio is too low
rationale: Grid cutoff causes: lateral decentering, upside-down focused grid, SID outside grid focusing range, tube angulation across grid lines. Results in loss of density (underexposure) on one or both sides.

stem: A Bucky mechanism:
options: Moves the grid during exposure to blur the grid lines from the image while maintaining scatter cleanup
rationale: Bucky (reciprocating grid): grid moves during exposure → grid lines blurred and not visible on image. Grid still absorbs scatter. Potter-Bucky diaphragm in the table or upright unit.

stem: Beam filtration in diagnostic radiography must have a minimum total of:
options: 2.5 mm aluminum equivalent for tubes operating above 70 kVp
rationale: Minimum total filtration: 2.5 mm Al equivalent for >70 kVp, 1.5 mm Al for 50-70 kVp. Includes inherent filtration (tube housing, glass envelope) + added filtration (Al sheets at tube port).

stem: The focal spot size affects image quality by:
options: Smaller focal spots producing sharper images with less geometric unsharpness, but having lower heat capacity
Larger focal spots producing sharper images
Having no effect on image sharpness
rationale: Focal spot: small (0.5-0.6 mm) for fine detail, high resolution. Large (1.0-1.2 mm) for high heat loading, routine work. Penumbra (geometric unsharpness) increases with larger FS, greater OID, and shorter SID.

stem: MRI produces images using:
options: Strong magnetic fields and radiofrequency pulses to excite hydrogen protons, whose signal is used to create images
rationale: MRI: static magnetic field (0.5-3T) aligns hydrogen protons. RF pulse tips protons from alignment. Protons release RF signal as they return to equilibrium. Signal differences between tissues create contrast.

stem: T1-weighted MRI images are characterized by:
options: Fat appearing bright (high signal) and water appearing dark (low signal), best for anatomical detail
Fat appearing dark and water appearing bright
rationale: T1-weighted: short TR, short TE. Fat = bright (short T1), water/CSF = dark (long T1). Good for anatomy, post-contrast enhancement (gadolinium shortens T1 → bright enhancement).

stem: T2-weighted MRI images are characterized by:
options: Water and fluid appearing bright (high signal) and fat appearing less bright, best for pathology detection
rationale: T2-weighted: long TR, long TE. Water/CSF = bright (long T2), fat = intermediate to dark. Edema, inflammation, tumors often have increased water content → bright on T2. Best for detecting pathology.

stem: Gadolinium-based contrast agents in MRI work by:
options: Shortening the T1 relaxation time of nearby tissues, causing them to appear brighter on T1-weighted images
rationale: Gadolinium (paramagnetic): shortens T1 → enhanced tissues appear bright on T1-weighted images. Used for tumor detection, vascular imaging, inflammation. Not nephrotoxic like iodine but linked to NSF in renal failure.

stem: The main safety concern unique to MRI is:
options: The strong magnetic field which can turn ferromagnetic objects into dangerous projectiles and affect implanted devices
rationale: MRI safety zones (1-4): projectile risk from ferromagnetic objects, implant heating/movement/malfunction, burns from RF energy, acoustic noise (up to 130 dB). Thorough screening required before entering Zone IV.

stem: Nephrogenic systemic fibrosis (NSF) is associated with:
options: Gadolinium-based contrast agents in patients with severe renal insufficiency (GFR <30)
rationale: NSF: rare, serious condition causing skin thickening and organ fibrosis. Linked to Group I GBCAs in patients with GFR <30. Screen renal function before gadolinium. Use Group II agents (lower risk) when necessary.

stem: MRI is contraindicated in patients with:
options: Most cardiac pacemakers (non-MR conditional), ferromagnetic aneurysm clips, cochlear implants (non-MR conditional), and metallic foreign bodies near vital structures
rationale: MRI contraindications: non-MR-conditional pacemakers/defibrillators, ferromagnetic cerebral aneurysm clips, metallic orbital foreign bodies, some cochlear implants. MR-conditional devices may be safe under specific conditions.

stem: The magnetic field strength of a typical clinical MRI scanner is:
options: 1.5 or 3.0 Tesla, which is 30,000-60,000 times stronger than Earth's magnetic field
rationale: Clinical MRI: 1.5T (most common) or 3.0T (higher SNR, faster scans). Research: up to 7T+. Earth's field: ~0.00005T. Higher field strength = higher SNR but more artifacts and SAR concerns.

stem: Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) in MRI is improved by:
options: Increasing field strength, larger voxel size, more signal averages (NEX), and using appropriate surface coils
Using larger matrix with thinner slices only
rationale: SNR improves: higher field strength, larger voxels (thicker slices, smaller matrix), more averages (NEX/NSA), surface coils, shorter TE. Trade-off: larger voxels reduce spatial resolution.

stem: FLAIR (Fluid Attenuated Inversion Recovery) MRI sequence:
options: Suppresses CSF signal while maintaining T2 contrast, making periventricular and cortical lesions more conspicuous
Is used only for musculoskeletal imaging
rationale: FLAIR: inversion recovery with TI set to null CSF. T2-like contrast but CSF is dark. Excellent for detecting MS plaques, stroke, subarachnoid hemorrhage, and cortical/periventricular lesions.

stem: Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) in MRI is most clinically important for:
options: Detecting acute stroke within minutes of onset by showing restricted water diffusion in ischemic brain tissue
rationale: DWI: measures random Brownian motion of water molecules. Acute ischemia → cytotoxic edema → restricted diffusion → bright on DWI, dark on ADC map. Most sensitive early indicator of acute stroke.

stem: MR angiography (MRA) can be performed:
options: With or without contrast using time-of-flight (TOF), phase contrast, or contrast-enhanced techniques
rationale: MRA techniques: TOF (inflow effect, no contrast needed), phase contrast (velocity encoding), contrast-enhanced (gadolinium bolus timing). Evaluates vascular anatomy, stenosis, aneurysms, and malformations.

stem: Specific absorption rate (SAR) in MRI refers to:
options: The rate at which RF energy is deposited in tissue, measured in watts per kilogram, which can cause tissue heating
rationale: SAR: RF energy absorption → tissue heating. FDA limit: whole body 4 W/kg, head 3.2 W/kg, extremity 12 W/kg over 15 minutes. Higher at 3T than 1.5T. Reduced by lower flip angles, longer TR.

stem: Gradient echo (GRE) sequences in MRI differ from spin echo by:
options: Using gradient reversal instead of a 180° refocusing pulse, allowing faster acquisition but increased susceptibility to magnetic field inhomogeneities
rationale: GRE: no 180° refocusing pulse → faster (shorter TR possible) but T2* weighting (sensitive to field inhomogeneity). Applications: fast imaging, angiography, hemorrhage detection (susceptibility effect).

stem: The quench in an MRI scanner refers to:
options: Rapid boiling of liquid helium coolant causing loss of superconductivity, magnet shutdown, and potential oxygen displacement in the scan room
rationale: Quench: sudden loss of superconductivity → rapid helium boil-off → large volumes of cold gas released into scan room. Risks: asphyxiation (O2 displacement), frostbite, pressure changes. Emergency ventilation required.

stem: Iodinated contrast agents are classified by osmolality as:
options: High-osmolar (HOCM), low-osmolar (LOCM), and iso-osmolar (IOCM), with lower osmolality agents having fewer adverse reactions
rationale: HOCM: ionic, ~1500 mOsm/kg (higher reaction rate). LOCM: non-ionic, ~600-800 mOsm/kg (fewer reactions, standard of care). IOCM: ~290 mOsm/kg (iodixanol). LOCM/IOCM preferred for all patients.

stem: The treatment for a severe anaphylactoid contrast reaction with hypotension and bronchospasm includes:
options: Epinephrine (1:1000 IM or 1:10,000 IV), IV fluids, oxygen, and monitoring — following ACR contrast reaction protocols
rationale: Severe reaction protocol: call code, epinephrine (IM 1:1000 for adults: 0.1-0.3 mL), IV access, fluids, O2, monitor vitals. Bronchospasm: albuterol inhaler. Laryngeal edema: epinephrine + consider intubation.

stem: Barium sulfate is used as a contrast agent for:
options: GI tract studies because it is insoluble, non-absorbable, and provides excellent mucosal coating
rationale: Barium sulfate: inert, insoluble, not absorbed from GI tract. Excellent mucosal coating for UGI, BE, and small bowel studies. Contraindicated if perforation suspected (use water-soluble contrast instead).

stem: Water-soluble contrast (e.g., Gastrografin) is used instead of barium when:
options: GI perforation is suspected, because barium leakage into the peritoneum causes severe peritonitis
rationale: Water-soluble contrast: used when perforation is suspected. Barium in peritoneum → severe chemical peritonitis. Water-soluble contrast is absorbed and excreted renally. Trade-off: poorer mucosal coating than barium.

stem: Contrast extravasation during IV injection can cause:
options: Local tissue injury ranging from mild swelling to compartment syndrome depending on volume and contrast type
rationale: Extravasation: contrast leaks into surrounding tissue. Mild: <10 mL, local swelling, elevation/ice. Severe: >50 mL, severe pain, compartment syndrome risk → surgical consultation. HOCM causes more tissue injury than LOCM.

stem: Premedication for contrast-allergic patients typically includes:
options: Corticosteroids (prednisone 50 mg at 13h, 7h, and 1h before) and diphenhydramine (50 mg 1h before), plus use of LOCM/IOCM
Only changing to a different contrast brand
rationale: Premedication protocol: prednisone 50 mg PO at 13, 7, and 1 hour before contrast. Diphenhydramine 50 mg PO/IV/IM 1 hour before. Use non-ionic LOCM or IOCM. Premedication reduces but does not eliminate risk.

stem: Metformin is held after iodinated contrast administration because:
options: Contrast-induced nephropathy could impair metformin clearance, leading to lactic acidosis in patients with renal insufficiency
rationale: Metformin: renally excreted. If CIN develops → metformin accumulates → lactic acidosis (rare but potentially fatal). Hold metformin 48 hours post-contrast. Check renal function before restarting.

stem: The power injector for CT contrast administration:
options: Delivers contrast at a precise, programmable flow rate and volume through IV access, typically 1-5 mL/second
rationale: Power injector: programmable rate (1-5 mL/s), volume, delay, and saline flush. Consistent bolus timing for optimal enhancement. Safety features: pressure limits, air detection. Requires adequate IV access (18-20 gauge).

stem: Oral contrast for CT abdomen/pelvis serves to:
options: Opacify the gastrointestinal tract to differentiate bowel loops from surrounding structures and pathology
rationale: Oral contrast: opacifies GI tract for abdominal CT. Positive contrast (dilute iodine or barium) appears bright. Negative contrast (water) for stomach/duodenal wall evaluation in pancreatic/gastric protocols.

stem: The ideal timing for contrast-enhanced CT scanning depends on:
options: The phase of enhancement desired (arterial, portal venous, delayed) which varies by organ and pathology being evaluated
rationale: Scan timing: arterial phase (20-35s post-injection) for arteries/hypervascular lesions. Portal venous (60-70s) for liver/abdominal organs. Delayed (5-15 min) for kidneys/ureters, urothelial lesions. Bolus tracking optimizes timing.

stem: Personnel dosimeters (TLD or OSL badges) should be worn:
options: At the collar level outside the lead apron (or two badges: collar outside + waist inside apron for fluoroscopy workers)
rationale: Single badge: collar level, outside apron (estimates head/neck dose and effective dose). Two-badge system (fluoroscopy): collar outside + waist under apron. Provides more accurate effective dose estimate.

stem: The concept of stochastic effects of radiation means:
options: The probability of occurrence increases with dose, but the severity is independent of dose — cancer and genetic effects are examples
Effects occur only above a threshold dose
Higher doses always produce more severe symptoms
rationale: Stochastic effects: random, probabilistic. No threshold dose (linear no-threshold model). Higher dose = higher probability, not higher severity. Examples: cancer induction, genetic mutations. Basis for dose limits.

stem: Deterministic effects of radiation:
options: Have a threshold dose below which they don't occur, and severity increases with dose above the threshold
rationale: Deterministic (tissue reactions): threshold exists. Above threshold, severity increases with dose. Examples: skin erythema (2 Gy), epilation (3-5 Gy), cataracts (2 Gy), sterility, radiation syndrome. Formerly called 'non-stochastic.'

stem: The linear no-threshold (LNT) model assumes:
options: Any dose of radiation, no matter how small, carries some risk of biological damage, with risk proportional to dose
There is a safe threshold below which no risk exists
rationale: LNT: conservative model used for radiation protection. No safe threshold. Risk = dose × risk coefficient. Basis for ALARA and dose limits. Debated but currently accepted by ICRP, NCRP, NRC.

stem: The dose equivalent limit for the lens of the eye for occupational workers is:
options: 150 mSv (15 rem) per year, though ICRP now recommends 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years
rationale: NCRP eye dose limit: 150 mSv/year. ICRP 118 (2011) recommended lowering to 20 mSv/year averaged over 5 years (max 50 mSv single year) based on cataract risk data. Lead glasses recommended in fluoroscopy.

stem: The embryo/fetus is most radiosensitive during:
options: Weeks 2-8 of gestation (organogenesis), when major organs are forming and radiation can cause congenital abnormalities
rationale: Radiation sensitivity by gestational age: pre-implantation (all-or-nothing), organogenesis (weeks 2-8, highest risk of malformations), fetal period (growth retardation, CNS effects). Threshold for effects: ~100-200 mGy.

stem: When imaging a pregnant patient, the technologist should:
options: Use the 10-day rule or confirm pregnancy status, apply maximum shielding, collimate tightly, optimize technique for lowest dose, and document the procedure
Use maximum mAs for best image quality
rationale: Pregnant patient imaging: verify pregnancy status, shield the abdomen if not in the primary beam, collimate tightly, use optimal technique (avoid repeats), consider alternative non-ionizing modalities (US, MRI).

stem: Structural shielding in radiology departments is designed based on:
options: Workload (W), use factor (U), occupancy factor (T), and the required dose limit for adjacent areas
rationale: Structural shielding design (NCRP 147): W = weekly workload (mA·min/week), U = fraction beam directed at barrier, T = occupancy of adjacent area, P = design dose limit. Primary vs. secondary barriers.

stem: The radiosensitivity of cells is greatest when they are:
options: Rapidly dividing, undifferentiated, and have a long mitotic future (Law of Bergonié and Tribondeau)
rationale: Bergonié and Tribondeau (1906): cells are more radiosensitive when they have high mitotic rate, long mitotic future, and are undifferentiated. Most sensitive: lymphocytes, spermatogonia, erythoblasts, intestinal crypt cells.

stem: Half-value layer (HVL) in radiation protection is used to:
options: Determine the quality of the x-ray beam and verify adequate filtration — it is the thickness of material needed to reduce beam intensity by half
rationale: HVL: measured in mm Al. Higher HVL = harder (more penetrating) beam = better filtration. Minimum HVL requirements ensure adequate filtration to remove low-energy photons that increase skin dose.

stem: Pediatric patients require special radiation protection considerations because:
options: Children are more radiosensitive than adults, have longer remaining lifetimes for cancer expression, and smaller body habitus requires technique adjustments
Children are less sensitive to radiation
Standard adult techniques are always appropriate
rationale: Pediatric considerations: higher radiosensitivity, longer lifetime for stochastic effects, smaller body → lower mAs needed. Image Gently campaign. Reduce kVp/mAs, tight collimation, shielding, avoid repeat exposures.

stem: The Image Gently campaign focuses on:
options: Raising awareness about reducing radiation dose in pediatric imaging through appropriate technique selection
Increasing radiation dose for better images
rationale: Image Gently (Alliance for Radiation Safety in Pediatric Imaging): advocates for child-size techniques, eliminating unnecessary exams, using alternative non-ionizing modalities, and educating families about radiation safety.

stem: Before performing any radiographic examination, the technologist must verify:
options: Patient identity (two identifiers), the ordered examination, clinical indication, and any relevant allergies or contraindications
rationale: Patient verification: name + DOB (or MRN), correct exam ordered, clinical indication matches body part, pregnancy status, allergies (contrast), isolation precautions, and relevant surgical/medical history.

stem: Standard precautions in radiology include:
options: Hand hygiene, PPE (gloves, gown, mask as needed), equipment disinfection, and proper handling of contaminated materials for all patients
Only for patients with known infections
No infection control measures are needed
rationale: Standard precautions: treat all patients as potentially infectious. Hand hygiene (before/after patient contact), gloves for body fluid contact, clean/disinfect equipment between patients, proper sharps disposal.

stem: For a patient experiencing a vasovagal reaction during an IV contrast injection, the technologist should:
options: Lower the head of the table (Trendelenburg position), monitor vital signs, establish IV access, and administer oxygen if needed
rationale: Vasovagal reaction: bradycardia, hypotension, diaphoresis, pallor. Treatment: Trendelenburg position, monitor vitals, IV fluids if hypotensive, atropine 0.6-1.0 mg IV for symptomatic bradycardia. Usually self-limiting.

stem: When transferring a patient from a wheelchair to the x-ray table, the technologist should:
options: Lock wheelchair brakes, use proper body mechanics, assist the patient to a standing position, and pivot to the table while supporting the patient
Push the patient onto the table
Let the patient transfer independently regardless of condition
rationale: Safe transfer: assess patient mobility, lock wheelchair, apply gait belt if needed, use proper body mechanics (bend knees, straight back), pivot with the patient, get help for immobile patients. Prevent falls.

stem: Informed consent for contrast administration requires:
options: Explaining the procedure, risks (allergic reaction, kidney injury), benefits, alternatives, and allowing the patient to ask questions
Only a written form without discussion
rationale: Informed consent elements: nature of procedure, risks (contrast reactions, CIN, extravasation), benefits (diagnostic information), alternatives (non-contrast studies, other modalities), and patient questions. Document appropriately.

stem: Oxygen delivery via nasal cannula typically provides:
options: 24-44% FiO2 at flow rates of 1-6 L/min, suitable for patients needing low to moderate supplemental oxygen
rationale: Nasal cannula: each 1 L/min increases FiO2 by ~4% above room air (21%). 1 L = 24%, 2 L = 28%, 3 L = 32%, 4 L = 36%, 5 L = 40%, 6 L = 44%. Well-tolerated, allows eating/talking.

stem: A patient in the radiology department suddenly becomes unresponsive. The first action should be:
options: Check responsiveness and breathing, call for help/activate the emergency response system, and begin CPR if no pulse is detected
Move the patient back to their room
Wait for a physician to arrive
rationale: Cardiac arrest response: check responsiveness, call code/911, begin high-quality CPR (30:2 ratio, 100-120 compressions/min, 2 inches deep), apply AED when available. Radiology staff should be BLS certified.

stem: Immobilization devices in pediatric radiography are used to:
options: Safely restrain the child during exposure to prevent motion blur and reduce the need for repeat exposures
rationale: Pediatric immobilization: Pigg-O-Stat, sandbags, tape, sponges, Velcro straps. Reduces motion → fewer repeats → lower cumulative dose. Parent may hold only if no other option (provide shielding and instruction).

stem: Pneumothorax on a chest radiograph appears as:
options: A thin white visceral pleural line with absence of lung markings peripheral to this line, best seen on an upright PA in expiration
rationale: Pneumothorax: air in pleural space. Look for visceral pleural line (thin white line) with no lung markings beyond it. Better seen on upright films and in expiration. Tension pneumothorax: mediastinal shift away from affected side.

stem: Congestive heart failure on chest radiograph may demonstrate:
options: Cardiomegaly, cephalization of vessels, Kerley B lines, pleural effusions, and perihilar haziness (bat-wing pattern)
rationale: CHF radiographic signs: cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio >0.5), cephalization (upper lobe vessel distention), Kerley B lines (interstitial edema), bilateral effusions, peribronchial cuffing, bilateral perihilar haziness.

stem: A tension pneumothorax is identified by:
options: Complete lung collapse with mediastinal shift toward the opposite side, tracheal deviation, and flattening/inversion of the hemidiaphragm
rationale: Tension pneumothorax: medical emergency. Progressive air accumulation → increased intrapleural pressure → complete lung collapse, contralateral mediastinal shift, hemodynamic compromise. Requires immediate needle decompression.

stem: Pneumonia on chest radiograph typically appears as:
options: Consolidation (opacification) in a lobar, segmental, or diffuse pattern with air bronchograms in the affected area
rationale: Pneumonia patterns: lobar (single lobe opacified), bronchopneumonia (patchy bilateral), interstitial (reticular pattern). Air bronchograms: air-filled bronchi visible within consolidated lung tissue.

stem: Osteoarthritis on radiographs is characterized by:
options: Joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, subchondral sclerosis, and subchondral cyst formation
rationale: OA radiographic features: JONS — Joint space narrowing (asymmetric), Osteophytes (bone spurs), Non-uniform sclerosis (subchondral), Subchondral cysts. Weight-bearing joints most affected. Asymmetric distribution.

stem: A Colles fracture is:
options: A distal radius fracture with dorsal displacement and angulation, commonly caused by a fall on an outstretched hand (FOOSH)
rationale: Colles fracture: distal radius with dorsal displacement/angulation. 'Dinner fork' deformity on lateral view. Common in elderly (osteoporosis) after FOOSH. Smith fracture is the reverse (volar displacement).

stem: Scoliosis is evaluated on radiographs by:
options: Measuring the Cobb angle on a standing full-spine AP radiograph to quantify the degree of lateral spinal curvature
rationale: Cobb angle: lines drawn along superior endplate of upper end vertebra and inferior endplate of lower end vertebra. Perpendiculars intersect at the Cobb angle. <10° = normal, 10-25° = mild, 25-40° = moderate, >40° = severe.

stem: Free air under the diaphragm on an upright chest or abdominal radiograph indicates:
options: Pneumoperitoneum, often due to bowel perforation, which is a surgical emergency
rationale: Pneumoperitoneum: free air in peritoneal cavity. Crescent of air under hemidiaphragm(s) on upright film. Causes: bowel perforation, post-surgical (expected for 7-10 days), peritoneal dialysis. Non-surgical causes exist.

stem: Small bowel obstruction (SBO) on abdominal radiographs shows:
options: Dilated small bowel loops (>3 cm), air-fluid levels on upright film, and decompressed distal colon
rationale: SBO signs: dilated small bowel (>3 cm), multiple air-fluid levels at different heights (step-ladder pattern), valvulae conniventes (mucosal folds span full width). Transition point may be visible. Distal colon is decompressed.

stem: A hip fracture in an elderly patient is most commonly located at:
options: The femoral neck or intertrochanteric region, often resulting from a low-energy fall in patients with osteoporosis
rationale: Elderly hip fractures: femoral neck (subcapital, transcervical, basicervical) or intertrochanteric (between greater and lesser trochanters). Risk: osteoporosis, fall. Femoral neck fractures may disrupt blood supply → avascular necrosis.

stem: CT artifact caused by patient motion appears as:
options: Blurring, streaking, or shading in the reconstructed image, which can be reduced by faster scan times and patient instruction
rationale: Motion artifacts: inconsistent data during acquisition → blurring, streaking. Remedies: fast rotation time, wide detector coverage, breath-hold instructions, immobilization, sedation for uncooperative patients.

stem: Metal artifacts in CT are caused by:
options: Severe beam hardening and photon starvation when x-rays pass through dense metallic objects, creating dark and bright streaks
rationale: Metal artifacts: streaks radiating from implants, dental fillings, surgical hardware. Caused by extreme attenuation → photon starvation + beam hardening. Reduced by: MAR algorithms, dual-energy CT, increasing kVp/mAs.

stem: Beam hardening artifacts in CT occur because:
options: Lower-energy photons are preferentially absorbed as the beam passes through dense tissue, shifting the average beam energy higher
The beam gets softer with depth
Too many photons reach the detector
rationale: Beam hardening: polychromatic beam → lower energies absorbed more → remaining beam has higher average energy. Artifacts: cupping (central darkening), dark bands between dense structures. Corrections: filtration, calibration, algorithms.

stem: Pitch in helical CT is defined as:
options: Table travel per gantry rotation divided by total collimation width — a pitch >1 means gaps between rotations
rationale: Pitch = table travel per rotation / total collimation. Pitch 1: no gap/overlap. Pitch >1: faster scan, lower dose, potential interpolation gaps. Pitch <1: overlap, higher dose, better z-resolution.

stem: Automatic tube current modulation (ATCM) in CT:
options: Adjusts mA based on patient attenuation in real-time to maintain consistent image quality while reducing dose
Keeps mA constant regardless of patient size
rationale: ATCM: angular (adjusts mA as tube rotates around patient — less mA in AP direction through thinner dimension) and longitudinal (adjusts mA along z-axis based on scout attenuation). Reduces dose 20-40%.

stem: Iterative reconstruction algorithms in CT:
options: Reduce image noise compared to filtered back projection, allowing lower radiation dose while maintaining image quality
Are slower and produce worse images
rationale: Iterative reconstruction: model-based noise reduction produces cleaner images at lower mAs. Allows 30-50% dose reduction vs. FBP. Types: ASIR, MBIR, SAFIRE, ADMIRE. Trade-off: different noise texture, longer reconstruction time.

stem: Dual-energy CT acquires data at two different kVp settings to:
options: Differentiate materials based on their energy-dependent attenuation, enabling virtual non-contrast images, material decomposition, and improved characterization
rationale: Dual-energy CT: two energy data sets → material decomposition (uric acid vs. calcium, iodine mapping), virtual non-contrast images, reduced metal artifacts, improved contrast-to-noise ratio. Applications: gout, renal stones, pulmonary embolism.

stem: CT angiography (CTA) for pulmonary embolism requires:
options: Precise bolus timing to opacify the pulmonary arteries, typically using bolus tracking with ROI in the main pulmonary artery
rationale: CTA PE: IV contrast 60-80 mL at 4-5 mL/s, bolus tracking with trigger in main PA (100-120 HU). Scan cranial-to-caudal during peak opacification. High sensitivity/specificity for PE. Evaluate through subsegmental branches.

stem: Size-specific dose estimates (SSDE) in CT account for:
options: Patient size by applying conversion factors to CTDIvol based on the patient's effective diameter, providing a more accurate individual dose estimate
rationale: SSDE: CTDIvol × size-dependent conversion factor. Smaller patients receive higher dose per unit CTDIvol. AAPM Report 204: measure patient diameter from scout or axial image. SSDE more clinically relevant than CTDIvol.

stem: CT perfusion imaging measures:
options: Blood flow, blood volume, mean transit time, and permeability of tissue by analyzing the time-density curve of contrast enhancement
rationale: CT perfusion: rapid sequential scanning during contrast bolus. Time-density curves analyzed: cerebral blood flow (CBF), cerebral blood volume (CBV), mean transit time (MTT). Used for acute stroke (ischemic penumbra) and tumor assessment.

stem: Detective quantum efficiency (DQE) of a digital detector measures:
options: The efficiency with which a detector converts x-ray exposure into a useful signal, accounting for noise — higher DQE means better image quality at lower doses
The physical size of the detector
rationale: DQE: ratio of output SNR² to input SNR². Ranges from 0 to 1 (higher = better). Flat panel DR detectors typically have higher DQE than CR. DQE is the most comprehensive measure of detector performance.

stem: Modulation transfer function (MTF) describes:
options: A detector's ability to accurately reproduce spatial frequencies from the object — higher MTF at high spatial frequencies indicates better spatial resolution
rationale: MTF: measures how faithfully a system reproduces input signal frequencies in the output image. Perfect system: MTF = 1 at all frequencies. Real systems: MTF decreases at higher frequencies. DR typically has better MTF than CR.

stem: Look-up tables (LUT) in digital radiography:
options: Map raw detector values to displayed grayscale values, allowing consistent image appearance and optimized contrast for different body parts
rationale: LUT: pre-programmed processing algorithms that optimize image display for specific examinations. Ensure consistent appearance regardless of exposure variations. Part of the vendor-specific processing chain.

stem: Edge enhancement in digital radiography:
options: Applies spatial frequency filtering to increase the visibility of edges and borders between structures, improving perceived sharpness
rationale: Edge enhancement: high-pass spatial frequency filter amplifies high-frequency components (edges). Improves perceived detail. Over-enhancement creates 'halo' artifacts. Adjustable parameter in most digital systems.

stem: The Nyquist frequency in digital imaging represents:
options: The maximum spatial frequency that can be accurately sampled, equal to 1/(2 × pixel pitch) — higher Nyquist frequency allows finer detail
rationale: Nyquist frequency = 1/(2×pixel size). For 0.1mm pixels: Nyquist = 5 lp/mm. Frequencies above Nyquist cause aliasing artifacts. Smaller pixels = higher Nyquist = better potential resolution.

stem: Windowing in CT (window width and window level) adjusts:
options: The range and center of Hounsfield units displayed as the visible grayscale, optimizing contrast for specific tissues
rationale: Window width (WW): range of HU displayed. Window level (WL): center HU value. Brain: WW 80, WL 35. Lung: WW 1500, WL -600. Bone: WW 2000, WL 400. Liver: WW 150, WL 60. Adjustable post-acquisition.

stem: Quantum noise (mottle) in radiography is caused by:
options: Insufficient x-ray photons reaching the detector, resulting in a grainy or speckled image appearance
rationale: Quantum mottle: statistical fluctuation in photon distribution. Worse at low mAs, high kVp (fewer interactions), thin body parts, high-speed imaging systems. Signal-to-noise ratio decreases with fewer photons.

stem: Image stitching in digital radiography is used for:
options: Combining multiple overlapping exposures into a single long image for scoliosis or full-leg length studies
rationale: Image stitching: multiple exposures combined into one seamless image. Applications: full-spine scoliosis evaluation, full-leg length studies for limb length discrepancy, and long bone surveys. Requires proper alignment.

stem: The right main bronchus is more vertical, shorter, and wider than the left, which is clinically significant because:
options: Aspirated foreign bodies are more likely to enter the right main bronchus due to its more vertical angle
The left bronchus receives more foreign bodies
The right lung is smaller than the left
rationale: Right main bronchus: wider, shorter, more vertical (25° from trachea vs. 45° for left). Foreign body aspiration → more commonly enters right bronchus → right lower lobe. Important for intubation positioning and aspiration pneumonia.

stem: The cardiac silhouette on a PA chest radiograph normally should not exceed:
options: 50% of the thoracic diameter (cardiothoracic ratio) — a ratio above this suggests cardiomegaly
rationale: Cardiothoracic ratio (CTR): maximum cardiac width / maximum thoracic width. Normal: ≤0.50 on PA. CTR >0.50 = cardiomegaly. AP projection magnifies the heart, making CTR unreliable.

stem: The sacroiliac joints are best demonstrated on:
options: AP axial with 25-30° cephalic angulation, or bilateral oblique projections (RPO/LPO at 25-30°)
rationale: SI joints: oblique orientation in body. AP axial: 25-30° cephalic angulation opens joint spaces. Obliques: 25-30° rotation (RPO shows right SI, LPO shows left SI). Important for inflammatory arthritis (ankylosing spondylitis).

stem: On a lateral cervical spine radiograph, the technologist should verify that:
options: All seven cervical vertebrae and the C7-T1 junction are demonstrated, with swimmer's view if C7-T1 is obscured by shoulders
Only C1-C3 need to be visible
The thoracic spine is the focus
rationale: Lateral C-spine: must demonstrate C1 through C7-T1 junction. If shoulders obscure lower cervical vertebrae: swimmer's lateral or CT. Trauma: horizontal beam lateral without moving the patient.

stem: The paranasal sinuses are best demonstrated on:
options: Waters (occipitomental), Caldwell (occipitofrontal), lateral, and submentovertex projections
rationale: Sinuses: Waters view (maxillary sinuses, OML 37° to IR), Caldwell (frontal/ethmoid sinuses, OML 15° to IR), lateral (all sinuses superimposed), SMV (sphenoid sinuses). Upright to show air-fluid levels.

stem: Cross-sectional anatomy at the level of the carina shows:
options: The bifurcation of the trachea into the right and left main bronchi, the aortic arch, SVC, and the esophagus posterior to the trachea
rationale: Carina level (T4-T5): tracheal bifurcation into main bronchi, aortic arch (or proximal descending aorta), SVC, esophagus (posterior to trachea), azygos vein arch. Important landmark for CT interpretation.

stem: The lateral view of the knee in true lateral position demonstrates:
options: Superimposition of the femoral condyles, with the patella in profile and the fibular head slightly posterior and inferior to the tibial plateau
rationale: True lateral knee: 5-7° cephalic CR, knee flexed 20-30°. Femoral condyles superimposed. Patella in profile. Evaluate: suprapatellar bursa (joint effusion), patellofemoral joint, fat pads (elevated = joint effusion).

stem: A patient presents to the ED after a motor vehicle accident with chest pain and shortness of breath. The portable chest radiograph shows a widened mediastinum. The most appropriate next step is:
options: Immediate CT angiography of the chest to evaluate for traumatic aortic injury
Repeat the chest x-ray in 24 hours
Discharge the patient with pain medication
rationale: Widened mediastinum after blunt trauma: suggests traumatic aortic injury (most common at isthmus). CTA is the gold standard for evaluation. Additional signs: left pleural effusion, tracheal deviation, depressed left main bronchus.

stem: A 65-year-old patient with renal insufficiency (eGFR 25) needs a CT scan. The radiologist orders a non-contrast study. The technologist should:
options: Proceed with the non-contrast CT as ordered, since no contrast is being given and there is no additional renal risk from the scan itself
Administer contrast anyway for better images
Require the patient to drink barium
rationale: Non-contrast CT: no iodinated contrast → no CIN risk. Proceed as ordered. If contrast were needed: discuss risk/benefit with radiologist, consider alternative (MRI with caution, US), hydrate if proceeding.

stem: During a barium enema, the patient reports sudden severe abdominal pain. The technologist should:
options: Stop the procedure immediately, do not remove the enema tip, notify the radiologist, and obtain appropriate radiographs to evaluate for perforation
rationale: Sudden pain during BE: suspect perforation. Stop barium instillation immediately. Do NOT remove enema tip (prevents further spillage). Notify radiologist. Images may show free air or contrast extravasation. Surgical consultation.

stem: A pediatric patient requires a chest x-ray but is crying and uncooperative. The best approach is:
options: Use immobilization devices (Pigg-O-Stat or board), obtain the image during inspiration, minimize repeat exposures, and reassure the child/parent
Sedate the child for a routine chest x-ray
Have multiple people hold the child with unshielded hands
Wait until the child is 18 to perform the exam
rationale: Pediatric chest technique: Pigg-O-Stat for upright PA/AP, catch inspiration during crying pause (deep breath before cry), short exposure time, child-appropriate mAs, tight collimation, shielding if possible.

stem: A patient with a known pacemaker presents for an MRI. The technologist should:
options: Verify whether the pacemaker is MR-conditional, check the manufacturer's conditions for safe scanning, and follow institutional protocol with cardiology involvement
Refuse all patients with pacemakers without exception
Proceed with the scan without any special precautions
Remove the pacemaker before the scan
rationale: MR-conditional pacemakers: can be safely scanned under specific conditions (field strength, SAR limits, lead configuration). Requires cardiology consultation, device interrogation before/after, continuous monitoring during scan.

stem: A patient develops urticaria (hives) and mild itching 5 minutes after receiving IV iodinated contrast. The appropriate response is:
options: Monitor the patient, administer diphenhydramine 25-50 mg PO/IM/IV as ordered, and observe for progression to more severe symptoms
Ignore the symptoms as they will resolve
rationale: Mild contrast reaction (urticaria, itching): observe, diphenhydramine 25-50 mg (PO/IM/IV). Monitor for 20-30 minutes for progression. Document the reaction. Most mild reactions are self-limiting. No epinephrine needed unless progressing.

stem: A lateral decubitus chest radiograph is ordered for a patient suspected of having a small right pleural effusion. The patient should be positioned:
options: Right side down (right lateral decubitus) with a horizontal x-ray beam to allow fluid to layer along the dependent chest wall
rationale: Decubitus for effusion: affected side DOWN. Fluid layers along dependent chest wall, confirming it is free-flowing. Minimum detectable: ~5-10 mL (vs. 200-300 mL on upright PA). If suspected pneumothorax: affected side UP.

stem: An overexposed digital radiograph appears clinically acceptable after processing. The technologist should:
options: Note the deviation index indicating overexposure and adjust technique for subsequent patients to apply ALARA principles, even though the current image looks acceptable
Take no action since the image looks fine
Delete the image and repeat with higher mAs
rationale: Digital 'dose creep': wide latitude masks overexposure (image appears acceptable). Check exposure indicator/deviation index. Positive DI = overexposure → higher dose than needed. Adjust technique downward. ALARA applies even when images look good.

stem: A CT scan of the chest shows a pulmonary embolism in the right main pulmonary artery. This is classified as:
options: A saddle embolus or central PE, which is a potentially life-threatening emergency requiring immediate clinical management
A minor finding requiring no follow-up
rationale: Central/saddle PE: clot at the bifurcation or in main pulmonary arteries. High risk of right heart strain, hemodynamic instability. Requires emergent treatment: anticoagulation, possible thrombolysis or thrombectomy.

stem: A patient presents for a wrist radiograph after a fall. The standard PA, lateral, and oblique views show no fracture, but the patient has anatomic snuffbox tenderness. The technologist should:
options: Communicate the clinical finding to the radiologist, as scaphoid fractures may be radiographically occult initially and additional views or follow-up imaging may be warranted
Tell the patient there is no fracture and discharge them
Perform a CT scan without a physician order
Repeat the same three views immediately
rationale: Anatomic snuffbox tenderness: high clinical suspicion for scaphoid fracture (most commonly fractured carpal bone). Initial radiographs may be negative in 15-20%. Scaphoid views, MRI, or follow-up radiographs in 10-14 days recommended.

stem: Which of the following are components of the primary radiation barrier in a radiographic room? (Select all that apply)
options: Lead-lined walls where the primary beam may be directed
Lead-lined floor if the room is above occupied spaces
The lead apron worn by the technologist
The Bucky tray under the table
rationale: Primary barriers: walls, floor, or ceiling that the primary (useful) beam may strike. Must be thicker than secondary barriers. The Bucky tray is part of the imaging system, not room shielding. Lead aprons protect personnel from scatter, not primary beam.

stem: Factors that increase patient radiation dose during CT include: (Select all that apply)
options: Higher mAs setting increasing tube current and exposure time
Lower pitch value causing greater overlap between rotations
Increased number of scan phases (e.g., multi-phase protocols)
rationale: CT dose factors: higher mAs (more photons), lower pitch (<1, overlapping data), narrower collimation (more rotations needed), multiple phases (repeated scanning). Also: higher kVp, reduced iterative reconstruction strength.

stem: Proper positioning for a PA projection of the wrist includes: (Select all that apply)
options: Patient seated with arm at shoulder level
Forearm pronated with palm flat on the image receptor
Central ray perpendicular to the midcarpal area
Fingers slightly flexed with fist clenched
rationale: PA wrist: seated, elbow flexed 90°, forearm pronated (palm down), wrist centered on IR, CR perpendicular to midcarpal area. Fingers should be relaxed/slightly flexed naturally, not clenched. Shoulder height positioning is for AP.

stem: Signs of a properly exposed and positioned PA chest radiograph include: (Select all that apply)
options: Ten posterior ribs visible above the diaphragm indicating full inspiration
Equal distance from clavicle ends to spinous processes indicating no rotation
Faint visualization of thoracic vertebrae through the cardiac shadow
Scapulae projected outside the lung fields
rationale: PA chest criteria: full inspiration (10 posterior ribs), no rotation (symmetric clavicle-spine distance), adequate penetration (faint thoracic vertebrae through heart), scapulae rotated out of lung fields, no apical cutoff.

stem: Advantages of using a grid in radiography include: (Select all that apply)
options: Reduction of scatter radiation reaching the image receptor
Improved image contrast especially for thick body parts
Allows use of higher kVp while maintaining contrast
Better visualization of fine anatomical detail in the chest and abdomen
rationale: Grid advantages: scatter cleanup → improved contrast, particularly for thick parts (>10 cm) where scatter is significant. Higher kVp can be used with grid maintaining acceptable contrast. Required: higher mAs to compensate for grid absorption.

stem: Risk factors for contrast-induced nephropathy include: (Select all that apply)
options: Diabetes mellitus especially with diabetic nephropathy
Dehydration at the time of contrast administration
Concurrent use of nephrotoxic medications such as NSAIDs or aminoglycosides
rationale: CIN risk factors: renal impairment, diabetes (especially with nephropathy), dehydration, nephrotoxic drugs, large contrast volumes, advanced age, heart failure, multiple myeloma. Prevention: hydration, low-osmolar contrast, minimize volume.

stem: MRI safety screening should include checking for: (Select all that apply)
options: Cardiac pacemakers or defibrillators and their MR-conditional status
Metallic foreign bodies especially orbital metal fragments
Cochlear implants and other implanted electronic devices
Pregnancy status especially in the first trimester
rationale: MRI screening: cardiac devices (pacemaker/ICD), metallic foreign bodies (orbital x-ray if history of metalwork), cochlear/other implants, aneurysm clips, pregnancy, claustrophobia, body piercings, tattoos, medication patches.

stem: Quality control tests performed daily on a mammography unit include: (Select all that apply)
options: Phantom image evaluation for minimum fiber, speck, and mass visibility
Processor quality control (if film-based) or flat-field uniformity (digital)
Visual inspection of compression paddle integrity
rationale: Daily mammography QC: phantom image (min 4 fibers, 3 speck groups, 3 masses), processor QC (sensitometry/densitometry for film), flat-field uniformity (digital), visual checks. Weekly/monthly: additional tests (compression force, artifact evaluation).

stem: Techniques to reduce pediatric radiation dose include: (Select all that apply)
options: Using pediatric-specific protocols with reduced mAs appropriate for body size
Tight collimation to the area of clinical interest
Using the fastest image receptor speed class available
Shielding radiosensitive organs when not in the primary field
rationale: Pediatric dose reduction: child-size techniques (lower mAs/kVp), tight collimation, shielding, fast receptors, immobilization (reduce repeats), Image Gently guidelines. Avoid unnecessary exams. Consider non-ionizing alternatives.

stem: Fluoroscopic dose reduction strategies include: (Select all that apply)
options: Using pulsed fluoroscopy at the lowest clinically acceptable pulse rate
Maximizing the SID to reduce skin dose per the inverse square law
Using last image hold to review anatomy without additional radiation
Minimizing magnification mode use as it increases dose
rationale: Fluoroscopy dose reduction: pulsed mode (lower pulse rate), last image hold, minimize magnification, tight collimation, virtual collimation, proper SID, reduce fluoroscopy time, appropriate kVp, and shielding.

stem: Wire mesh tool testing in radiography is used to evaluate:
options: Screen-film contact in cassettes — poor contact appears as areas of blurriness on the test image
rationale: Wire mesh test: uniform fine mesh placed on cassette, exposed. Areas of poor screen-film contact appear blurred compared to areas with good contact. Poor contact → unsharpness in that area of the image. Replace cassette if >1 cm defect.

stem: Reject analysis in a radiology department:
options: Tracks and categorizes repeated or rejected radiographs to identify patterns and reduce unnecessary exposures
Is performed only once per year
rationale: Reject analysis: categorize rejected images (positioning errors, exposure errors, motion, artifacts, patient-related). Identify trends, provide targeted education, reduce repeat rates. Goal: <2% repeat rate. Reduces patient dose and department cost.

stem: The star pattern test tool is used to measure:
options: Effective focal spot size by imaging a star-shaped test tool and measuring the blur pattern
rationale: Star pattern (resolution test): radiograph of star test tool. The blur zone diameter relates to focal spot size via known formula. Smaller blur zone = smaller focal spot. Alternative: pinhole or slit camera methods. Annual testing required.

stem: Sensitometry and densitometry in film processing QC:
options: Use a sensitometer to create a standardized exposure on film and a densitometer to measure the resulting optical densities, tracking processor consistency
Are only needed for digital systems
rationale: Sensitometry: controlled light exposure to film strip (21 steps). Densitometry: measure optical density of each step. Plot characteristic curve. Monitor speed, contrast, fog indices daily. Detect processor drift before affecting clinical images.

stem: Linearity testing of the x-ray tube verifies that:
options: The radiation output is proportional to the mAs setting — doubling the mAs should double the exposure
The focal spot size is correct
rationale: Linearity: mR/mAs should be consistent (coefficient of variation <0.05) across different mA stations at the same kVp. If not linear: mA station malfunction, space charge compensation error, or timer inaccuracy.

stem: The target theory of radiation biology states that:
options: DNA is the critical target for radiation damage, and a direct hit on DNA can cause cell death, mutation, or carcinogenesis
Cell membranes are the only targets
rationale: Target theory: DNA is the critical target. Direct effect: ionizing radiation directly damages DNA strands. Indirect effect: radiation ionizes water → free radicals → damage DNA. Double-strand breaks are most lethal. Single-strand breaks are usually repairable.

stem: Free radicals produced by the indirect effect of radiation:
options: Are highly reactive molecules (especially hydroxyl radical OH•) that damage DNA and other cellular macromolecules
Are produced only by direct effect
rationale: Indirect effect: radiation + H₂O → H• + OH• (free radicals). OH• is most damaging. Free radicals break chemical bonds in DNA. Indirect effect accounts for ~70% of biological damage from low-LET radiation (x-rays, gamma rays).

stem: The oxygen enhancement ratio (OER) describes:
options: The increased radiosensitivity of cells in the presence of oxygen — oxygenated cells are 2-3 times more radiosensitive than hypoxic cells
The decreased effect of radiation with oxygen
No relationship between oxygen and radiation damage
rationale: OER: dose in hypoxia / dose in oxygen to produce same effect. OER ~2.5-3.0 for low-LET radiation. Oxygen fixates free radical damage (makes it permanent). Hypoxic tumor cells are more radioresistant → clinical challenge in radiation therapy.

stem: Early (acute) effects of high-dose whole-body radiation exposure include:
options: Hematopoietic syndrome (1-10 Gy), gastrointestinal syndrome (10-50 Gy), and cerebrovascular syndrome (>50 Gy)
rationale: Acute radiation syndrome: hematopoietic (bone marrow failure, 2-6 weeks, potentially survivable), GI (intestinal lining destruction, 3-10 days, often fatal), cerebrovascular (cerebral edema, hours to days, uniformly fatal).

stem: The relative biological effectiveness (RBE) compares:
options: The biological effectiveness of different types of radiation — alpha particles have a much higher RBE than x-rays
rationale: RBE: dose of reference radiation (250 kVp x-rays) / dose of test radiation to produce the same biological effect. X-rays/gamma: RBE ~1. Neutrons: RBE ~10. Alpha particles: RBE ~20. Higher LET = higher RBE (generally).

stem: Computed radiography (CR) differs from direct radiography (DR) in that CR:
options: Uses a separate photostimulable phosphor plate that must be processed in a reader, while DR uses a fixed detector that provides immediate image display
Provides faster image display than DR
rationale: CR: portable plates → insert in reader → laser stimulated emission → digital image. Slower workflow. DR: fixed/wireless panel → immediate image display. DR is replacing CR due to speed, DQE, and workflow advantages.

stem: Ghosting artifact in CR occurs when:
options: A previous image is incompletely erased from the phosphor plate, leaving a faint residual pattern on the subsequent image
The laser power is too high
rationale: CR ghosting: incomplete plate erasure. Previous exposure pattern partially retained. Causes: insufficient erase cycle, damaged phosphor plate, excessive time between exposure and reading. Solution: re-erase plate, replace damaged plates.

stem: Dead pixels on a flat panel DR detector:
options: Appear as consistently bright or dark spots on every image and are corrected by the detector's calibration map — excessive dead pixels require detector replacement
rationale: Dead pixels: defective detector elements. Dead pixel map: system interpolates values from surrounding pixels. If dead pixel count exceeds manufacturer threshold, detector needs replacement. Daily flat-field calibration detects new dead pixels.

stem: The exposure indicator in a Carestream DR system is called:
options: EI (Exposure Index) — a target EI value is set for each exam, and the deviation index (DI) shows how actual exposure compares to the target
rationale: Exposure indicators vary by vendor: EI/DI (IEC standard), S-number (Fuji — inversely proportional), EI (Carestream), lgM (Siemens). IEC standardized: EI = c × (dose to detector). DI = 10 × log₁₀(EI/EI_target). DI = 0 is ideal.

stem: PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System) in radiology:
options: Stores, retrieves, distributes, and displays digital medical images throughout the healthcare enterprise using DICOM standards
rationale: PACS: replaces film-based storage. Components: imaging modalities, network, archive server, workstations. Uses DICOM for image format/transfer. Integrates with HIS/RIS via HL7. Enables remote reading, prior comparison, and workflow optimization.

stem: The Towne projection (AP axial skull) demonstrates:
options: The occipital bone, foramen magnum, and dorsum sellae projected within the foramen magnum shadow
rationale: Towne (AP axial): CR angled 30° caudal to OML (or 37° to IOML). Demonstrates: occipital bone, foramen magnum, dorsum sellae within foramen magnum, petrous ridges. Used for skull fractures and sella turcica evaluation.

stem: The Waters projection (parietoacanthial) is used to demonstrate:
options: The maxillary sinuses with the petrous ridges projected below the sinuses by extending the patient's chin
rationale: Waters view: OML 37° to IR (chin extended). Projects petrous ridges below maxillary sinuses. Best for: maxillary sinuses, orbital floors (blowout fractures), facial bones. Upright to demonstrate air-fluid levels.

stem: For a lateral projection of the lumbar spine:
options: The patient is in a true lateral position with knees flexed, a support placed under the waist to align the spine, and CR directed to L3
The CR is directed caudally at 30°
Both arms are raised above the head
rationale: Lateral lumbar: true lateral, knees/hips flexed for comfort and stability. Support under waist to maintain horizontal alignment. CR perpendicular to L3 (iliac crest). Collimate to include L1-L5 and surrounding soft tissues.

stem: AP pelvis radiograph positioning requires:
options: Patient supine, legs internally rotated 15-20° to place the femoral necks in profile, with the CR directed midway between the ASIS and pubic symphysis
rationale: AP pelvis: supine, legs internally rotated 15-20° (places femoral necks parallel to IR, greater trochanters less superimposed). CR to midpoint between ASIS and pubic symphysis. Gonadal shielding when it won't obscure anatomy.

stem: The Judet views of the acetabulum include:
options: Internal and external oblique projections (45°) to demonstrate the anterior and posterior acetabular columns and walls
rationale: Judet views: 45° internal oblique (demonstrates anterior column + posterior wall) and 45° external oblique (demonstrates posterior column + anterior wall). Used for acetabular fracture classification and surgical planning.

stem: For an AP projection of the foot:
options: The CR is angled 10° posteriorly (toward the heel) to open the tarsometatarsal joints
The CR is perpendicular to the foot
rationale: AP foot: 10° posterior angulation (toward heel) opens tarsometatarsal joints. Shows metatarsals, phalanges, and proximal tarsal articulations. Alternative: dorsoplantar projection with foot flat on IR.

stem: The scaphoid (navicular) view of the wrist requires:
options: Ulnar deviation of the wrist with CR angled 10-15° proximally to elongate the scaphoid and open the scaphoid fat stripe
rationale: Scaphoid view: PA with ulnar deviation (extends scaphoid out from under radius), CR angled 10-15° toward elbow. Some protocols: Stecher method (wrist elevated 20° on sponge). Best demonstrates scaphoid fractures.

stem: The inlet and outlet views of the pelvis are used to evaluate:
options: Pelvic ring fractures — inlet shows AP displacement, outlet shows superior/inferior displacement
rationale: Inlet view: CR angled 40° caudal, shows pelvic ring from above (AP displacement). Outlet view: CR angled 40° cephalic, shows sacrum/lower pelvis from below (vertical displacement). Essential for pelvic trauma evaluation.

stem: For an AP internal oblique view of the elbow:
options: The elbow is rotated 45° medially to demonstrate the coronoid process and trochlea in profile
rationale: Internal oblique (medial) elbow: 45° medial rotation from AP position. Demonstrates: coronoid process free of superimposition, trochlea in profile, olecranon process within trochlear notch. Useful for coronoid fractures.

stem: The tangential (sunrise/skyline) projection of the patella demonstrates:
options: The patellofemoral joint and the articular surface of the patella for evaluation of subluxation, fractures, or chondromalacia
rationale: Tangential patella: knee flexed, x-ray beam tangent to patellofemoral joint. Demonstrates patellar position (lateral subluxation), patellar fractures, patellofemoral arthritis. Merchant method: patient supine, knees flexed 40-45° over table edge.

stem: The purpose of wearing two dosimetry badges (dual-badge system) during fluoroscopy is to:
options: More accurately estimate the effective dose — one badge at the collar outside the apron measures unshielded dose, one at the waist under the apron measures shielded dose
rationale: Dual-badge system: collar badge (outside apron) captures head/neck/eye dose. Waist badge (under apron) captures trunk dose behind shielding. Algorithms combine both to calculate total effective dose more accurately than single badge.

stem: Optically stimulated luminescence (OSL) dosimeters use:
options: Aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) crystals that, when stimulated by laser light, emit luminescence proportional to the absorbed radiation dose
rationale: OSL (e.g., Luxel badge): Al₂O₃:C crystal. Laser stimulation releases trapped electrons → luminescence proportional to dose. Advantages over TLD: re-readable, wider dose range, greater sensitivity (~1 mrem), can be re-analyzed.

stem: The NRC (Nuclear Regulatory Commission) or agreement state regulates:
options: The use of radioactive materials (nuclear medicine, radiation therapy sources) but NOT x-ray producing equipment, which is regulated by individual states
