rationale: NRC: radioactive materials (byproduct, source, special nuclear material). X-ray equipment: regulated by individual state radiation control programs. FDA: regulates equipment manufacturing and performance standards.

stem: The dose limit for the skin (shallow dose) for occupational workers is:
rationale: NCRP occupational dose limits: whole body (effective) 50 mSv/year, lens of eye 150 mSv/year, skin (shallow) 500 mSv/year, extremities (hands/feet) 500 mSv/year. All based on preventing deterministic effects.

stem: As Low As Diagnostically Acceptable (ALADA) modifies the ALARA concept by:
options: Emphasizing that dose reduction should not compromise diagnostic image quality — the lowest dose that produces a diagnostically acceptable image
rationale: ALADA: refinement of ALARA. Acknowledges that excessively reducing dose can degrade image quality, leading to non-diagnostic studies and potential repeats. Balance: minimum dose for clinically adequate images. Quality and safety together.

stem: Ionic contrast agents differ from non-ionic agents in that ionic agents:
options: Dissociate into charged particles in solution, have higher osmolality, and carry a higher risk of adverse reactions
rationale: Ionic agents: dissociate into cation + anion → higher osmolality → more chemotoxic effects (pain, nausea, hemodynamic changes). Non-ionic: do not dissociate, lower osmolality, fewer adverse effects. Non-ionic is standard of care.

stem: Delayed contrast reactions can occur:
options: Hours to days after contrast administration and may include skin rashes, urticaria, and flu-like symptoms
rationale: Delayed reactions: 1 hour to 7 days post-injection. Most common: skin rash, urticaria, itching. Usually self-limiting. Risk factors: prior delayed reaction, IL-2 therapy. Treatment: antihistamines, topical steroids.

stem: The maximum recommended dose of iodinated contrast for an adult patient is approximately:
options: 300-350 mg iodine per kg of body weight, though clinical protocols typically use much less
rationale: Contrast dose guidelines: typical CT dose 100-150 mL of 300-370 mgI/mL concentration. Maximum: ~300-350 mgI/kg body weight. Higher doses increase CIN risk. Use lowest effective volume. Consider patient weight and renal function.

stem: Rectal contrast for CT is used primarily to:
options: Opacify the rectum and sigmoid colon to distinguish bowel from pelvic pathology such as abscesses or masses
rationale: Rectal contrast: dilute iodinated contrast via enema. Opacifies distal colon to differentiate from pelvic masses, abscesses, or post-surgical collections. Used in trauma, appendicitis, and pelvic pathology protocols.

stem: Air as a negative contrast agent is used in:
options: Double-contrast barium studies of the GI tract and double-contrast arthrography to distend the joint and coat surfaces
MRI as an alternative to gadolinium
rationale: Air/CO₂ as negative contrast: appears black (radiolucent). Double-contrast BE: barium coats mucosa, air distends lumen → mucosal detail. Double-contrast arthrography: air + iodine distend joint. CO₂ angiography: for contrast-allergic patients.

stem: Image receptor sensitivity (speed class) in radiography:
options: Higher speed class systems require less radiation for adequate exposure but may have more noise, while lower speed systems produce higher quality images at higher doses
Higher speed always means better image quality
Speed class is only relevant for CT
All detectors have the same sensitivity
rationale: Speed class: S200 (standard), S400 (faster), S800 (fastest). Higher speed = less exposure needed = lower dose but potentially more noise. CR: speed class affects plate response. DR: detector sensitivity is inherent. Match speed to clinical need.

stem: Mobile (portable) radiography requires additional safety precautions including:
options: Maintaining maximum possible distance from the tube during exposure, wearing a lead apron, and ensuring no unnecessary personnel are in the immediate area
Standing directly next to the tube
rationale: Portable radiography: no structural shielding. Technologist: lead apron, maximize distance (cord length ≥6 feet), stand perpendicular to scatter (90° to beam direction), announce exposure, clear area of non-essential personnel.

stem: Proper marker placement on radiographs is important because:
options: Right (R) and left (L) anatomical markers must be placed on the image before exposure to correctly identify patient laterality and prevent surgical errors
They can be added digitally after exposure without consequence
Only one marker is needed for all exams
rationale: Anatomical markers: must be placed on IR before exposure (not added digitally). Correct side identification prevents wrong-side surgery. Include R/L, technologist initials, date. Legal requirement. Digital annotation alone is not acceptable.

stem: The National Patient Safety Goals relevant to radiology include:
options: Using two patient identifiers, reducing healthcare-associated infections, improving medication safety, and identifying safety risks
No specific safety goals apply to radiology
rationale: Joint Commission National Patient Safety Goals applicable to radiology: two patient identifiers (name + DOB/MRN), infection prevention (hand hygiene, equipment cleaning), falls prevention, contrast/medication safety, radiation dose management.

stem: The purpose of the lead glass window in a radiographic control booth is to:
options: Allow the technologist to observe the patient during exposure while maintaining radiation protection through lead-equivalent shielding
Provide natural light to the room
Allow the patient to see the technologist's computer
It serves no radiation protection purpose
rationale: Control booth: lead-equivalent walls + lead glass window (minimum 1.5 mm Pb equivalent). Technologist can observe patient during exposure without radiation exposure. Lead glass thickness must match wall shielding requirements.

stem: HIPAA regulations in radiology require:
options: Protection of patient health information including images, reports, and any identifying data — access limited to authorized personnel with clinical need
No restrictions on sharing patient images
Sharing all images publicly for education
rationale: HIPAA in radiology: protect PHI (images, reports, demographics). Limit access to authorized users. Secure PACS access (login credentials, audit trails). Anonymize images for teaching/research. Report breaches. Includes electronic, paper, and verbal PHI.

stem: During a contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen, the patient reports warmth and a metallic taste. The technologist should:
options: Reassure the patient that these are normal expected sensations from IV iodinated contrast injection and continue monitoring
rationale: Normal contrast sensations: warmth (especially in groin/perineum), metallic taste, mild nausea. These are NOT allergic reactions. Reassure patient, continue monitoring. Differentiate from true reactions: urticaria, dyspnea, hypotension, facial swelling.

stem: A radiograph of the knee shows adequate positioning but is too dark (overexposed). The most appropriate correction is:
options: Decrease the mAs to reduce the number of x-ray photons reaching the detector
rationale: Overexposure (too dark/dense): reduce mAs (primary density control). 30% mAs change produces visible density difference. Alternative: decrease kVp by 5-10%. In digital: adjust processing, but correct at source for ALARA compliance.

stem: Effective dose (measured in mSv or rem) represents:
options: The sum of weighted equivalent doses in all tissues and organs, accounting for both radiation type and tissue radiosensitivity to estimate overall biological risk
Only the absorbed dose to one organ
rationale: Effective dose: E = Σ(wT × HT). Tissue weighting factors (wT) reflect relative radiosensitivity. Gonads: 0.08, breast: 0.12, lung: 0.12, stomach: 0.12, colon: 0.12, bone marrow: 0.12. Allows comparison of different exam types.

stem: The lateral cervical spine radiograph should be performed with:
options: A 72-inch SID to minimize magnification, the patient in an upright or cross-table lateral position, and the CR directed to C4
rationale: Lateral C-spine: 72-inch SID (reduces magnification of OID). Upright: patient looks straight ahead, depress shoulders. Trauma: horizontal beam, do not move patient. CR to C4 (thyroid cartilage level). Include C1 through C7-T1.

stem: PET/CT combines:
options: Metabolic information from positron emission tomography with anatomical detail from CT, providing fused functional and structural images
Two CT scans at different energies
rationale: PET/CT: PET shows metabolic activity (FDG uptake in hypermetabolic tissues like cancer) fused with CT anatomy. Applications: cancer staging, recurrence detection, treatment response monitoring, cardiac viability, neurological disorders.

stem: Bone densitometry (DEXA) uses:
options: Two different x-ray energies to differentiate bone mineral content from soft tissue, measuring bone mineral density (BMD) for osteoporosis assessment
rationale: DEXA (Dual-energy X-ray Absorptiometry): two x-ray energies separate bone from soft tissue. BMD measured at lumbar spine and proximal femur. T-score: ≥-1 = normal, -1 to -2.5 = osteopenia, ≤-2.5 = osteoporosis.

stem: Magnification radiography deliberately increases OID to:
options: Enlarge small structures for better visualization, using a small focal spot to minimize geometric unsharpness
Eliminate the need for a grid
rationale: Magnification technique: increased OID → magnified image. Small focal spot (0.1-0.3 mm) essential to maintain sharpness. Direct air gap eliminates need for grid (scatter misses detector). Applications: mammography, extremities, angiography.

stem: Tube angulation in radiography:
options: Shifts the position of anatomical structures on the image and can be used to project overlying structures off of the area of interest
Has no effect on the image
rationale: Tube angulation: cephalic or caudal tilting of the CR. Used to: open joint spaces (AP cervical spine: 15-20° cephalic), project anatomy away from overlying structures, and compensate for body part curvature.

stem: Automatic exposure control (AEC) in radiography terminates the exposure when:
options: A predetermined amount of radiation has reached the detector, ensuring consistent image density regardless of patient thickness variations
The timer reaches a preset value
rationale: AEC (phototimer/ionization chamber): radiation sensors behind the patient/before the receptor. When detector receives adequate exposure → circuit terminates mA. Technologist selects: kVp, detector(s), density control, and backup time.

stem: The backup timer on an AEC system:
options: Provides a safety cutoff to prevent excessive patient exposure if the AEC fails to terminate, typically set at 150% of the expected mAs
rationale: Backup timer: safety feature required by regulation. Terminates exposure if AEC fails (e.g., incorrect chamber selected, chamber not covered). Prevents extreme overexposure. Set at 150% of expected mAs or 600 mAs maximum.

stem: The mAs reciprocity law states:
options: The same mAs value will produce the same radiation output regardless of the mA and time combination used (e.g., 100 mA × 0.1s = 200 mA × 0.05s = 10 mAs)
Higher mA always produces a different exposure than higher time
The law only applies to digital systems
mAs has no relationship to radiation output
rationale: Reciprocity law: output depends on total mAs, not individual mA or time. 100 mA × 0.1s = 200 mA × 0.05s = 10 mAs = same exposure. Choose higher mA with shorter time to reduce motion blur when possible.

stem: Geometric unsharpness (penumbra) in radiography is minimized by:
options: Using a small focal spot, increasing SID, and decreasing OID to reduce magnification and blur
rationale: Geometric unsharpness (Ug): Ug = (focal spot × OID) / (SID - OID). Minimize by: smaller focal spot, greater SID, smaller OID. Also minimize motion blur: short exposure time, immobilization.

stem: The 15% rule in radiography states:
options: Increasing kVp by 15% has approximately the same effect on image density as doubling the mAs, and can be used to reduce patient dose
Decreasing kVp by 15% doubles the density
The 15% rule applies only to CT
kVp changes do not affect density
rationale: 15% rule: increase kVp by 15% → can halve mAs and maintain similar density. Higher kVp reduces contrast (wider gray scale). Used for dose reduction. Reverse: decrease kVp by 15%, double mAs for higher contrast (at higher dose).

stem: Contrast resolution in radiography refers to:
options: The ability to distinguish between tissues with small differences in density (attenuation), and is improved by reducing scatter and optimizing kVp
The ability to see fine lines and edges
The number of pixels in the image
rationale: Contrast resolution: distinguishing similar-density tissues. Improved by: appropriate kVp selection, grids (reduce scatter), collimation, optimal processing. CT has superior contrast resolution compared to radiography due to tomographic nature.

stem: Cinefluorography (cine) is primarily used in:
options: Cardiac catheterization to record real-time moving images of cardiac anatomy and coronary arteries at high frame rates
rationale: Cine: high-frame-rate acquisition (15-30 fps) during cardiac catheterization. Records coronary anatomy, wall motion, valve function, and interventional procedures. Higher dose than standard fluoroscopy.

stem: A T-tube cholangiogram is performed to:
options: Evaluate the biliary ducts for retained stones after cholecystectomy by injecting contrast through a T-shaped drainage tube
rationale: T-tube cholangiogram: post-cholecystectomy, contrast injected through T-tube under fluoroscopy. Demonstrates bile duct anatomy, retained stones, strictures, and free flow into duodenum.

stem: During a small bowel follow-through (SBFT), images are taken at intervals to:
options: Track barium transit through the small intestine and evaluate for obstruction, masses, inflammation, or structural abnormalities
rationale: SBFT: patient drinks barium, timed images (every 15-30 minutes) track transit through duodenum, jejunum, ileum to terminal ileum/cecum. Evaluates transit time, mucosal pattern, caliber, and pathology (Crohn's, obstruction, tumors).

stem: The modified barium swallow study (MBS/videofluoroscopic swallow study) is supervised by:
options: A speech-language pathologist in collaboration with a radiologist to evaluate oropharyngeal swallowing function
rationale: MBS: collaborative study between SLP and radiologist. Evaluates oral, pharyngeal, and upper esophageal swallow phases. Different food consistencies tested. Identifies aspiration, penetration, and dysphagia causes.

stem: Defecography is a fluoroscopic study that evaluates:
options: Rectal and pelvic floor function during defecation using barium paste to diagnose pelvic floor disorders
rationale: Defecography: barium paste instilled into rectum. Fluoroscopy during rest, squeeze, and straining/defecation. Evaluates rectocele, intussusception, pelvic floor descent, and puborectalis function.

stem: Hysterosalpingography (HSG) is performed to evaluate:
options: The uterine cavity and fallopian tube patency by injecting contrast through the cervix under fluoroscopic guidance
rationale: HSG: water-soluble contrast injected through cervical cannula. Fluoroscopy demonstrates uterine cavity shape (fibroids, polyps, anomalies) and tubal patency (fill and spill of contrast). Performed in infertility workup.

stem: During a myelogram, contrast is injected into:
options: The subarachnoid space (thecal sac) of the spinal canal to outline the spinal cord and nerve roots
rationale: Myelography: non-ionic iodinated contrast injected intrathecally via lumbar puncture. Fluoroscopy with tilting table moves contrast to visualize spinal cord, nerve roots, and thecal sac. Often followed by CT (CT myelogram).

stem: The radiation dose to the patient during fluoroscopy can be reduced by:
options: Positioning the x-ray tube as far from the patient as possible and the image receptor as close to the patient as possible
Placing the tube close to the patient's skin
Moving the image receptor away from the patient
rationale: Geometry optimization: tube far from patient (reduces entrance dose), receptor close to patient (reduces magnification → ABC doesn't increase output). Tube-to-skin distance minimum: 30 cm (fixed), 38 cm (mobile).

stem: Fluoroscopy time tracking is required because:
options: Cumulative fluoroscopy time directly correlates with patient radiation dose, and prolonged procedures can cause deterministic skin injuries
It is a billing requirement only
Time has no relationship to dose
rationale: Fluoro time tracking: 5-minute audible alarm (FDA requirement), cumulative dose tracking recommended. Extended procedures (>60 min) risk skin injuries. Sentinel event at 15 Gy cumulative skin dose.

stem: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) works by:
options: Subtracting a pre-contrast (mask) image from contrast-enhanced images to remove bone and soft tissue, isolating the contrast-filled vessels
rationale: DSA: mask image (before contrast) subtracted from contrast images. Result: only contrast-filled structures visible. Superior vessel visualization without bone/soft tissue overlay. Requires patient cooperation (no motion between mask and contrast images).

stem: Enteral feeding tube placement verification using fluoroscopy:
options: Provides real-time visualization of tube advancement and confirmation of correct positioning in the stomach or small bowel
rationale: Fluoroscopic tube placement: real-time guidance for nasogastric, nasoenteric, or gastrostomy tubes. Confirms tip position (stomach, duodenum, or jejunum). Avoids malposition complications (bronchial placement, esophageal coiling).

stem: Fluoroscopic-guided lumbar puncture is performed when:
options: Bedside attempts have failed or the patient has anatomy that makes blind placement difficult, such as obesity or spinal deformity
rationale: Fluoroscopic LP: needle advanced under real-time guidance. Biplane fluoroscopy helps confirm needle position in the thecal sac. Used for difficult anatomy, failed bedside attempts, or when injecting contrast (myelography).

stem: Roadmapping in vascular fluoroscopy:
options: Overlays a subtracted angiographic image on the live fluoroscopy to guide catheter and wire navigation through complex vessel anatomy
Replaces the need for contrast injection
Is a type of CT reconstruction
rationale: Roadmapping: reference angiographic image superimposed on live fluoroscopy. Allows real-time navigation through vasculature without repeated contrast injections. Reduces both contrast volume and fluoroscopy time.

stem: Cone beam CT (CBCT) in fluoroscopy suites:
options: Uses a C-arm rotational acquisition to create cross-sectional images without transferring the patient to a dedicated CT scanner
Is identical to standard diagnostic CT
rationale: CBCT: C-arm rotates around patient (typically 200° arc), flat panel detector captures projections. Reconstruction creates CT-like cross-sectional images. Used in interventional procedures for real-time 3D guidance.

stem: Fluoroscopy room design requires:
options: Structural shielding for all walls, lead glass control barrier, ventilation system, and radiation warning signs at all entrances
rationale: Fluoroscopy room design: lead-lined walls/floor/ceiling (thickness per NCRP 147), control area with lead glass window, radiation caution signs, dosimetry storage, protective equipment storage, emergency equipment access.

stem: Breast density is categorized by the ACR into:
options: Four categories: A (almost entirely fatty), B (scattered fibroglandular), C (heterogeneously dense), D (extremely dense)
rationale: ACR breast density: A (almost entirely fatty, <25% dense), B (scattered fibroglandular, 25-50%), C (heterogeneously dense, 51-75%), D (extremely dense, >75%). Categories C and D reduce mammographic sensitivity.

stem: The CC (craniocaudal) mammographic view should demonstrate:
options: All medial breast tissue, most lateral tissue, nipple in profile or at the center, and fat posterior to the fibroglandular tissue
rationale: CC positioning criteria: medial tissue completely included, maximum lateral tissue, nipple in profile (or centered), pectoralis muscle at posterior edge (seen in ~30% of CCs), no skin folds, adequate compression.

stem: Suspicious mass characteristics on mammography include:
options: Irregular or spiculated margins, heterogeneous density, associated calcifications, and architectural distortion
rationale: Suspicious mass features: spiculated margins (stellate), irregular shape, high density relative to breast tissue, associated microcalcifications, architectural distortion. Benign features: round/oval, well-circumscribed, fat-containing (lipoma, oil cyst).

stem: Supplemental screening with breast MRI is recommended for women who:
options: Have a lifetime breast cancer risk of 20% or greater, including those with BRCA mutations, prior chest radiation, or strong family history
rationale: Breast MRI screening: recommended for high-risk women (≥20% lifetime risk). Includes BRCA carriers, Li-Fraumeni syndrome, prior chest radiation (10-30 years old), and calculated high risk. MRI has higher sensitivity but lower specificity than mammography.

stem: Automatic exposure control (AEC) in mammography adjusts technique based on:
options: Compressed breast thickness and composition, selecting optimal kVp and mAs to achieve consistent image quality
rationale: Mammography AEC: sensor measures transmitted radiation. Thicker/denser breast → AEC increases mAs and may adjust kVp (or switch to Rh filter). Ensures consistent optical density/signal regardless of breast variation.

stem: The rolled view in mammography is performed to:
options: Determine the true location of a lesion by rolling breast tissue to separate overlapping structures that mimic a mass
rationale: Rolled views: upper breast tissue rolled one direction, lower tissue rolled the opposite. If the 'lesion' moves with the rolled tissue, it is superimposition (not a true mass). If it persists in the same location, it is a real finding.

stem: Benign calcification patterns on mammography include:
options: Popcorn (fibroadenoma), eggshell/rim (oil cyst/fat necrosis), vascular (parallel tracks), large rod-like (ductal ectasia), and round/scattered
rationale: Benign calcifications: typically benign = BI-RADS 2. Popcorn (degenerating fibroadenoma), eggshell/rim (fat necrosis), vascular (arterial walls), large rod-like (secretory disease), round scattered (lobular). No follow-up needed.

stem: The cleavage view (CV/FB) in mammography demonstrates:
options: Medial breast tissue bilaterally in a single image, useful for evaluating lesions in the extreme medial breast
rationale: Cleavage view: both breasts placed on the IR simultaneously, compression applied. Demonstrates extreme medial tissue. Used when a lesion is medially located and not well seen on standard CC view.

stem: Skin markers in mammography are used to:
options: Identify the location of palpable findings, skin lesions, or surgical scars so they can be correlated with mammographic findings
rationale: Skin markers: radiopaque markers placed on palpable lumps, moles, scars, or nipples. Allows correlation between clinical findings and mammographic appearance. Different marker shapes indicate different finding types.

stem: The tangential view in mammography is performed to:
options: Demonstrate whether a finding is within the breast parenchyma or on/near the skin surface by imaging the area tangentially
rationale: Tangential (TAN) view: x-ray beam tangent to the area of interest. Differentiates skin lesions (dermal calcifications, moles) from intramammary findings. Useful for confirming skin calcifications are truly dermal.

stem: CT of the head without contrast is the first imaging study for:
options: Suspected acute stroke to rapidly differentiate hemorrhagic from ischemic stroke, guiding treatment decisions
rationale: Non-contrast head CT: first-line for acute stroke. Hemorrhagic stroke appears hyperdense (bright) immediately. Ischemic stroke may not be visible for 6-12 hours. Rules out hemorrhage before thrombolysis. Fast (seconds), widely available, 24/7.

stem: High-resolution CT (HRCT) of the chest uses:
options: Thin slices (1-1.25 mm), sharp reconstruction kernel, and prone imaging to evaluate interstitial lung disease patterns
Soft tissue kernel for lung evaluation
rationale: HRCT: thin slices (1-1.25 mm), high-spatial-frequency algorithm (sharp/bone kernel), increased matrix. Demonstrates fine lung parenchymal detail: honeycombing, ground glass, traction bronchiectasis. Prone images differentiate dependent atelectasis from true disease.

stem: CT coronary angiography (CCTA) requires:
options: ECG-gating to synchronize image acquisition with the cardiac cycle, beta-blocker to slow heart rate, and precise contrast bolus timing
rationale: CCTA: prospective or retrospective ECG-gating. Target HR <65 bpm (beta-blockers). Nitroglycerin for coronary vasodilation. Precise bolus timing (ROI in ascending aorta). Evaluates coronary artery stenosis, calcium scoring.

stem: Virtual colonoscopy (CT colonography) is performed using:
options: Low-dose CT with air or CO₂ distension of the colon, followed by 2D and 3D reconstructions to evaluate for polyps
rationale: CT colonography: colon cleansed (bowel prep), distended with CO₂ (auto-insufflator). Low-dose CT in supine and prone positions. 3D fly-through (virtual endoscopy) + 2D review. Screening for polyps ≥6 mm.

stem: CT-guided biopsy uses cross-sectional imaging to:
options: Guide needle placement into a target lesion for tissue sampling, providing precise real-time visualization of needle position relative to anatomy
Only image the lesion without intervention
rationale: CT-guided biopsy: intermittent CT scans verify needle advancement toward target. Used for lung, liver, kidney, bone, and soft tissue lesions. Advantages: precise targeting, avoids vital structures. Risks: pneumothorax (lung), hemorrhage.

stem: Streak artifacts from dental amalgam in head CT can be reduced by:
options: Tilting the gantry to avoid the plane of the dental work, increasing kVp/mAs, and using metal artifact reduction algorithms
rationale: Dental artifact reduction: gantry tilt (angle away from dental plane), increase kVp (reduces beam hardening), MAR algorithms (iterative correction), dual-energy CT (virtual monoenergetic images at higher keV).

stem: The split-bolus CT technique is used to:
options: Combine two phases of contrast enhancement into a single scan acquisition, reducing the number of scan passes and overall radiation dose
rationale: Split bolus: contrast divided into two injections separated by a time delay. Single scan captures both phases (e.g., nephrographic + excretory). Reduces scan passes, dose, and examination time. Used in CT urography.

stem: Low-dose CT lung cancer screening (LDCT) is recommended for:
options: Adults aged 50-80 with a 20+ pack-year smoking history who currently smoke or quit within the past 15 years
All adults regardless of smoking history
rationale: LDCT screening (USPSTF 2021): age 50-80, ≥20 pack-year history, current smoker or quit ≤15 years ago. Low-dose technique: reduced mAs, no contrast. Annual screening reduces lung cancer mortality by 20-33%.

stem: Multiplanar reformation (MPR) in CT:
options: Reconstructs images in coronal, sagittal, or oblique planes from the original axial volumetric data set without additional radiation
Requires a new scan for each plane
rationale: MPR: volumetric (isotropic) axial data reformatted into any plane. Coronal and sagittal reconstructions standard for most body CT. Oblique reformats for specific anatomy (acetabulum, spine). No additional dose.

stem: CT calcium scoring is used to:
options: Quantify coronary artery calcification as a predictor of cardiovascular disease risk using non-contrast ECG-gated CT
rationale: Calcium scoring: non-contrast, ECG-gated CT of the heart. Agatston score quantifies coronary calcification. Score 0 = very low risk, 1-100 = mild, 101-400 = moderate, >400 = high risk for cardiovascular events.

stem: CT enterography differs from a standard abdominal CT by:
options: Using a large volume of neutral oral contrast (water or polyethylene glycol) to distend the small bowel for detailed mucosal evaluation
rationale: CT enterography: 1-1.5L neutral oral contrast (water, VoLumen, PEG) over 45-60 min distends small bowel. IV contrast at portal venous phase. Evaluates Crohn's disease activity, strictures, fistulas, and small bowel tumors.

stem: 3D volume rendering in CT:
options: Assigns colors and opacity values to different tissue densities to create photorealistic three-dimensional images from volumetric CT data
Cannot be performed on standard CT data
rationale: Volume rendering: assigns opacity/color to HU ranges. Shows spatial relationships of structures. Applications: surgical planning (facial reconstruction), vascular mapping (pre-transplant), orthopedic (fracture assessment).

stem: Renal stone CT protocol (CT KUB) uses:
options: Non-contrast, low-dose technique to detect urinary tract calculi regardless of composition, as nearly all stones are visible on CT
rationale: CT KUB: non-contrast, low-dose. Detects all stone types (calcium, uric acid, struvite, cystine) — unlike radiography which misses radiolucent stones. Also identifies hydronephrosis, perinephric stranding, and alternative diagnoses.

stem: Maximum intensity projection (MIP) in CT:
options: Displays the highest attenuation value along each ray through the volume, useful for visualizing contrast-filled vessels and calcifications
rationale: MIP: projects maximum HU along viewing ray. Vessels (contrast-filled) and calcifications are highlighted. Used in CT angiography, pulmonary nodule detection, and vascular assessment. Complementary to volume rendering.

stem: Pediatric CT dose reduction strategies include:
options: Weight-based or age-based protocol selection, reduced kVp, ATCM, iterative reconstruction, and limiting the number of scan phases
Using adult protocols for all children
Increasing dose for better image quality
rationale: Pediatric CT dose reduction: Image Gently Step Lightly. Size-specific protocols, reduced kVp (80-100 kVp for children), ATCM, iterative reconstruction, limit phases (single phase when possible), scan only indicated body region.

stem: The Grashey view of the shoulder demonstrates:
options: The glenohumeral joint space in profile by rotating the patient 35-45° toward the affected side
rationale: Grashey (true AP shoulder): patient rotated 35-45° toward affected side, placing the scapular body parallel to the IR. Opens the glenohumeral joint space. Demonstrates joint congruity, glenoid labrum, Hill-Sachs lesion.

stem: The scapular Y view is used to evaluate:
options: Anterior or posterior shoulder dislocation by positioning the humeral head relative to the glenoid fossa in a lateral scapular projection
rationale: Scapular Y (lateral scapula): patient rotated 45-60° from lateral. Scapular body in profile with acromion, coracoid, and body forming a 'Y'. Humeral head should project over the glenoid center. Displacement indicates dislocation.

stem: The West Point view of the shoulder demonstrates:
options: The anteroinferior glenoid rim to evaluate for Bankart lesion after anterior shoulder dislocation
rationale: West Point: patient prone, arm abducted 90°, forearm hanging off table. CR angled 25° anterior and 25° medial. Demonstrates anteroinferior glenoid rim (Bankart fracture) and Hill-Sachs defect of humeral head.

stem: For a lateral wrist radiograph, the wrist is positioned:
options: In a true lateral position with the ulnar surface of the wrist against the IR, elbow flexed 90°, and fingers slightly flexed
With the palm flat on the IR
In full extension with radial surface down
rationale: Lateral wrist: ulnar side down, true lateral (no rotation). Elbow flexed 90°. CR perpendicular to the wrist joint. Demonstrates: carpal alignment, lunate (moon shape), scaphoid profile, dorsal/volar displacement of fractures.

stem: The carpal tunnel view (Gaynor-Hart) demonstrates:
options: The carpal tunnel and its bony borders by imaging the wrist in hyperextension with the CR angled 25-30° toward the hand
rationale: Carpal tunnel: wrist hyperextended, palm flat on IR (or table). CR angled 25-30° to the long axis of the hand entering at the base of the palm. Demonstrates: carpal tunnel, hook of hamate, pisiform, trapezium ridge.

stem: AP weight-bearing bilateral knee radiographs are performed to:
options: Evaluate joint space narrowing under physiologic load, which may not be apparent on non-weight-bearing views
rationale: Weight-bearing knees: standing AP (or PA Rosenberg at 45° flexion). Demonstrates true joint space narrowing under load — more sensitive for early osteoarthritis than supine AP. Used for surgical planning (total knee arthroplasty).

stem: The tunnel view (Camp-Coventry) of the knee demonstrates:
options: The intercondylar fossa, intercondylar eminence, and posterior articular surfaces of the femoral condyles
rationale: Tunnel view: patient prone, knee flexed 40-50°, CR angled 40-50° caudal to the popliteal fossa. Demonstrates intercondylar fossa (loose bodies), tibial eminences, and posterior femoral condyle surfaces.

stem: The Harris-Beath calcaneal view demonstrates:
options: The calcaneus in an axial projection, showing the sustentaculum tali, tuberosity, and subtalar joint
rationale: Harris-Beath (plantodorsal calcaneus): patient standing on IR or dorsiflexed foot on IR, CR angled 45° cephalad to base of 3rd metatarsal. Demonstrates calcaneal fractures (Böhler's angle assessment), subtalar coalition.

stem: The frog-leg lateral hip projection demonstrates:
options: The proximal femur and hip joint in a modified lateral position with the knee flexed and thigh abducted 45°
rationale: Frog leg lateral: patient supine, knee flexed, thigh abducted 45°, plantar surface of foot against opposite knee. CR to femoral neck. Demonstrates femoral head, neck, trochanteric region in modified lateral. NOT for trauma.

stem: For a PA oblique chest (RAO position) during a cardiac series:
options: The patient is rotated 45° with the right anterior side against the IR, and barium is swallowed to outline the esophagus and left atrium
The patient faces away from the IR
rationale: RAO chest (45°): demonstrates left atrium (posterior heart border) against barium-filled esophagus. Left atrial enlargement causes posterior displacement of esophagus. Part of the cardiac series with LAO, lateral, and PA views.

stem: The axial (Lorenz) view of the calcaneus is performed by:
options: Dorsiflexing the foot with the CR angled 40° cephalad, entering at the base of the 3rd metatarsal
rationale: Axial calcaneus: patient supine or seated, foot dorsiflexed. CR angled 40° cephalad entering plantar surface at base of 3rd metatarsal. Demonstrates calcaneal body, tuberosity, sustentaculum tali, and subtalar joint.

stem: The apical lordotic chest view is used to demonstrate:
options: The lung apices free of clavicular superimposition, useful for evaluating apical lesions such as Pancoast tumors
rationale: Lordotic view: patient leans back against IR (AP lordotic) or angles CR 15-20° cephalad. Projects clavicles above lung apices. Demonstrates apical pathology (tuberculosis, Pancoast tumor) free of clavicular/first rib superimposition.

stem: The Zanca view of the AC (acromioclavicular) joints:
options: Uses a 10-15° cephalic tube angle with reduced exposure compared to standard shoulder technique to profile the AC joint space
Requires the patient to be prone
rationale: Zanca view: AP projection with 10-15° cephalic CR tilt. Reduced technique (1/3 to 1/2 of standard shoulder technique) since AC joint is superficial. Best demonstrates AC joint space for separation, arthritis, or distal clavicle osteolysis.

stem: Upright abdominal radiographs are preferred over supine for evaluating:
options: Air-fluid levels, which indicate bowel obstruction, ileus, or free intraperitoneal fluid — visible only with a horizontal x-ray beam
rationale: Upright abdomen: horizontal beam demonstrates air-fluid levels (step-ladder pattern in SBO), free air under diaphragm. Supine views show distribution pattern of gas but cannot demonstrate air-fluid levels.

stem: The coned-down lateral L5-S1 spot view is performed because:
options: Standard lateral lumbar views often underexpose the L5-S1 region due to the dense iliac bone superimposition, requiring a dedicated higher-technique exposure
It replaces the AP lumbar view
rationale: L5-S1 spot: coned lateral centered on L5-S1 with increased technique. Iliac crests superimpose on L5-S1 on standard lateral → insufficient penetration. Spot view uses smaller field, higher mAs. CR 5-8° caudal to L5-S1.

stem: Computed radiography (CR) phosphor plates should be erased:
options: Before use if the plate has been idle for more than 48 hours, to clear accumulated background radiation exposure
Only when the image appears foggy
rationale: CR plates accumulate background radiation over time. If idle >48 hours, background signal degrades image quality (fog/noise). Erase before use to clear accumulated exposure. Daily QC checks should include plate evaluation.

stem: The dose-length product (DLP) for a CT examination is calculated as:
options: CTDIvol multiplied by the scan length in centimeters, providing an estimate of total energy deposited during the scan
rationale: DLP = CTDIvol × scan length (cm). Units: mGy·cm. Used to estimate effective dose using body-region conversion factors (k-factors). Head CT DLP ~1000 mGy·cm, chest CT ~400 mGy·cm, abdomen CT ~700 mGy·cm (typical).

stem: Kerma-area product (KAP) meters in radiography measure:
options: The product of air kerma and beam area at the measurement point, providing a comprehensive patient dose metric independent of distance
rationale: KAP (also called DAP): transmission ionization chamber mounted on the collimator. Measures kerma × area. Independent of distance from source. Provides total radiation burden to patient. Used for dose tracking and audit.

stem: In mammography, the posterior nipple line (PNL) is measured from:
options: The nipple to the pectoralis muscle (or edge of image) and should be within 1 cm on CC vs MLO views to confirm adequate positioning
The skin line to the nipple
The compression paddle to the detector
rationale: PNL: drawn perpendicular from the nipple to the pectoralis muscle (or posterior image edge on CC). Measures amount of posterior tissue included. CC and MLO PNL should be within 1 cm. If not, additional views may be needed.

stem: The chair-back position for lumbar oblique views at 45° demonstrates the:
options: Zygapophyseal (facet) joints and the pars interarticularis of the lumbar vertebrae (Scotty dog appearance)
rationale: Lumbar oblique (45°): demonstrates zygapophyseal joints. 'Scotty dog': eye=pedicle, ear=superior articular process, front leg=inferior articular process, body=lamina, neck=pars interarticularis. Defect in neck = spondylolysis.

stem: The primary radiation interaction at mammographic energies (22-32 kVp) is:
options: Photoelectric absorption, which provides high contrast between breast tissue types due to the strong dependence on atomic number
rationale: At mammographic energies (17-25 keV effective), photoelectric effect dominates. Photoelectric ∝ Z³/E³. Provides excellent soft tissue contrast between fat, fibroglandular tissue, and calcifications. Compton becomes more significant at higher energies.

stem: Noise power spectrum (NPS) in digital radiography describes:
options: The frequency distribution of noise in the image, providing a more complete noise characterization than simple standard deviation measurements
rationale: NPS: describes noise variance as a function of spatial frequency. Provides information about noise texture (grain pattern). Different from standard deviation which is a single number. Used in comprehensive detector performance evaluation.

stem: During a barium enema, insufflation with air creates a:
options: Double-contrast study that coats the mucosal surface with barium while air distends the lumen, providing detailed mucosal visualization
rationale: Double-contrast BE: barium coats mucosa (white coating on walls), air distends lumen (dark center). Demonstrates polyps, mucosal lesions, diverticula. Superior mucosal detail compared to single-contrast (barium-filled). More technically demanding.

stem: The image lag (persistence) artifact in fluoroscopy:
options: Appears as ghosting or trailing of moving structures and is more common in flat panel detectors using amorphous silicon than amorphous selenium
rationale: Image lag: residual signal from previous frame persists into current frame. Appears as ghosting/trailing of rapidly moving objects. More pronounced in indirect flat panel (a-Si) systems. Reduced by faster frame rates and improved detector designs.

stem: The Seldinger technique in interventional radiology involves:
options: Percutaneous vessel access using a needle, guidewire, and catheter exchange over the wire — the standard method for vascular catheterization
rationale: Seldinger technique (1953): needle punctures vessel → guidewire inserted through needle → needle removed → catheter threaded over wire → wire removed. Foundation of modern vascular interventional procedures.

stem: When imaging a patient with a chest tube, the radiograph should demonstrate:
options: The entire length of the chest tube, its tip position, and the presence or absence of residual pneumothorax or effusion
The chest tube is not important on the image
rationale: Post-chest tube radiograph: verify tube position (should be within pleural space, not in fissure), tip direction (anterior for pneumothorax, posterior/dependent for effusion), and effectiveness (resolution of pneumothorax/effusion).

stem: A patient with a suspected cervical spine injury should be imaged:
options: Without moving the neck, using a cross-table lateral with a horizontal beam as the initial radiographic view
By flexing and extending the neck
rationale: Cervical spine trauma: do NOT move the neck. Cross-table lateral (horizontal beam): first view, taken without removing collar. Must include C1-C7/T1. If cervical fracture is suspected, CT is the standard of care for definitive evaluation.

stem: The normal Böhler's angle of the calcaneus is:
options: 20-40 degrees — a decreased angle suggests a calcaneal compression fracture
rationale: Böhler's angle: angle between two lines on lateral calcaneus. Line 1: anterior process to posterior facet peak. Line 2: posterior facet peak to posterior tuberosity. Normal: 20-40°. <20° = compression fracture.

stem: Osteoporosis on radiographs is characterized by:
options: Generalized decrease in bone density, thinning of cortices, trabecular resorption, and increased susceptibility to insufficiency fractures
rationale: Osteoporosis: generalized bone loss. Radiographic signs: cortical thinning, trabecular resorption (vertical trabeculae preserved longer), vertebral compression fractures, Colles fractures. However, 30-40% bone loss required before radiographically visible — DEXA is more sensitive.

stem: Paget's disease on radiographs shows:
options: Thickened, enlarged bones with coarsened trabecular pattern, cortical thickening, and mixed lytic/sclerotic phases
rationale: Paget's disease: three phases. Lytic (osteoclastic resorption), mixed (lytic + blastic), sclerotic (osteoblastic — thickened, dense bone). 'Cotton wool' skull, picture frame vertebrae, enlarged bone. Complications: fractures, sarcomatous degeneration.

stem: Pleural effusion on a chest radiograph appears as:
options: Blunting of the costophrenic angle on upright film, with larger effusions causing a meniscus sign and opacification of the lower lung field
rationale: Pleural effusion: fluid in pleural space. Upright PA: blunted costophrenic angle (first sign, ~200-300 mL). Larger: meniscus sign (concave upward curve), opacification. Massive: complete hemithorax opacification with mediastinal shift.

stem: COPD/emphysema on chest radiograph shows:
options: Hyperinflated lungs with flattened diaphragms, increased AP diameter, increased retrosternal air space, and decreased/attenuated vascular markings
rationale: Emphysema radiographic signs: hyperinflation (>10 posterior ribs, >6 anterior ribs), flat diaphragms, barrel chest (lateral: increased AP diameter), increased retrosternal space, attenuated peripheral vascular markings, bullae.

stem: An avulsion fracture occurs when:
options: A fragment of bone is pulled away from the main bone by a tendon or ligament during forceful muscle contraction or sudden stretch
A direct blow causes a transverse fracture
Bone dies from lack of blood supply
rationale: Avulsion fracture: tendon/ligament attachment pulls bone fragment away. Common sites: anterior tibial spine (ACL avulsion), base of 5th metatarsal (peroneus brevis), ASIS (sartorius), ischial tuberosity (hamstrings). Often seen in athletes.

stem: A greenstick fracture is characterized by:
options: An incomplete fracture where one cortex is broken and the other is bent, occurring in children whose bones are more flexible than adults
rationale: Greenstick: pediatric incomplete fracture. One cortex breaks, opposite cortex bends but remains intact. Due to greater elasticity of growing bone. Other pediatric fracture types: buckle/torus (cortical buckling without break), bowing deformity.

stem: Salter-Harris fractures involve:
options: The growth plate (physis) in pediatric patients, classified into five types based on the relationship of the fracture to the physis, epiphysis, and metaphysis
rationale: Salter-Harris: Type I (through physis only), II (physis + metaphysis — most common), III (physis + epiphysis), IV (through metaphysis, physis, and epiphysis), V (crush/compression of physis). SALTR mnemonic: Slipped, Above, Lower, Through, Rammed.

stem: The purpose of post-processing in digital radiography includes:
options: Optimizing image appearance through windowing/leveling, edge enhancement, noise reduction, and histogram analysis without additional radiation
rationale: Digital post-processing: grayscale mapping (look-up tables), edge enhancement, noise reduction, histogram equalization, stitching, subtraction. Optimizes display without additional exposure. Cannot compensate for fundamentally inadequate technique.

stem: The histogram in digital radiography represents:
options: The distribution of pixel values in the image, used by the system to identify the exposed anatomy and apply appropriate processing
The radiation dose to the patient
rationale: Histogram: graph of pixel values (x-axis) vs. frequency (y-axis). System analyzes histogram to identify collimation borders, useful signal range, and anatomy type. Errors in histogram analysis (e.g., collimation issues) cause processing failures.

stem: Exposure creep in digital radiography refers to:
options: The gradual increase in radiation exposure over time because digital detectors can produce acceptable images at higher-than-necessary doses
rationale: Exposure creep: digital latitude masks overexposure → technologists may not recognize excessive dose. Images look fine but patient receives unnecessarily high dose. Prevention: monitor exposure indicators, set target values, establish protocols.

stem: The purpose of spatial frequency filtering in digital image processing is to:
options: Selectively enhance or suppress different levels of image detail — high-pass filters enhance edges while low-pass filters reduce noise
rationale: Spatial frequency filtering: high-pass (edge enhancement, sharpening), low-pass (smoothing, noise reduction), band-pass (selective frequency enhancement). Applied mathematically to the image data without affecting dose.

stem: The anode angle affects the x-ray beam by:
options: Determining the effective focal spot size, the field coverage at a given SID, and the magnitude of the anode heel effect
Having no effect on the image
rationale: Anode angle: steeper angle (smaller °) → smaller effective focal spot (better resolution) but limited field coverage and more pronounced heel effect. Typical: 7° (angiography), 12-15° (general radiography), 17° (large field coverage).

stem: The space charge effect in an x-ray tube:
options: Limits tube current at low kVp settings because the electron cloud around the filament repels additional electrons from leaving the cathode
Increases tube output at all kVp
rationale: Space charge effect: negative electron cloud near filament repels emitted electrons back. At low kVp: insufficient potential to overcome space charge → limited mA. At high kVp: space charge overcome, tube operates at saturation current (mA determined by filament heating).

stem: Off-focus radiation (extra-focal radiation) is produced when:
options: Electrons strike areas of the anode other than the focal track, producing radiation from a larger effective source and degrading image sharpness
rationale: Off-focus (extra-focal) radiation: electrons strike anode outside focal track → x-rays from larger area → penumbra/unsharpness. Reduced by: proper tube design, filtration, beam-limiting devices placed close to the tube.

stem: Photon counting detector technology in CT:
options: Directly converts individual x-ray photons to electrical signals with energy discrimination, offering higher spatial resolution and material decomposition without dual-source scanning
Uses the same technology as current detectors
Is not used in clinical settings
rationale: Photon counting detectors: each photon detected individually with energy measurement. Advantages: higher spatial resolution (smaller pixels), spectral information (multi-energy bins), reduced electronic noise, lower dose potential. FDA approved (Siemens NAEOTOM Alpha).

stem: Artificial intelligence (AI) in radiology is being used for:
options: Computer-aided detection/diagnosis, automated measurements, workflow prioritization (stroke, PE), and image quality optimization
rationale: AI in radiology: CADe/CADx (detecting/diagnosing findings), triage (flagging critical findings for urgent reading), auto-segmentation, dose optimization, quality assurance. Augments, not replaces, radiologist and technologist expertise.

stem: The ten-day rule refers to:
options: Scheduling elective radiographic examinations of the abdomen/pelvis during the first 10 days of the menstrual cycle when pregnancy is least likely
A 10-day waiting period after contrast administration
rationale: Ten-day rule: elective abdominal/pelvic radiography during days 1-10 of menstrual cycle (before ovulation). Minimizes risk of irradiating an early embryo. Some facilities use a '28-day rule' (imaging any time if menses is confirmed). Ask pregnancy status regardless.

stem: A chest radiograph performed AP portable differs from PA upright because:
options: AP projection magnifies the heart and mediastinum, has a shorter SID increasing magnification, and the patient is often supine which redistributes pleural fluid and air
rationale: AP portable limitations: shorter SID (40-48" vs 72"), AP beam → heart magnified (cannot use CTR), supine → fluid layers posteriorly (may miss effusions), mediastinum widened, poor inspiration (supine diaphragm elevation).

stem: The lateral projection of the facial bones demonstrates:
options: The superimposed facial bones including the maxilla, nasal bones, zygoma, and mandible, with the sella turcica visible posteriorly
rationale: Lateral facial bones: superimposed bilateral structures. Demonstrates: nasal bones, anterior nasal spine, maxilla, mandible body/ramus, floor of orbit/sella turcica. Useful for nasal fractures, anterior wall of maxillary sinus.

stem: MRI claustrophobia management includes:
options: Patient education before the scan, feet-first positioning when possible, music/mirror/prism glasses, anxiolytic medication, and open MRI as alternative
Forcing the patient into the scanner
rationale: Claustrophobia management: pre-scan education (describe sounds, duration), coaching/breathing techniques, feet-first entry, eye covers/mirrors, music, anxiolytics (diazepam/lorazepam with escort), open/wide-bore MRI scanners. ~5-10% of patients experience significant claustrophobia.

stem: The use of bismuth shielding over radiosensitive organs during CT:
options: Has been debated — while it reduces surface dose to organs like the breast and thyroid, it can increase noise and affect image quality near the shielding
Is universally recommended by all organizations
rationale: Bismuth shielding (breast, thyroid, lens): reduces surface dose 30-40% but increases scatter/noise near the shield. AAPM recommends ATCM and organ-based dose modulation as alternatives. If used: place after scout scan (doesn't affect ATCM).

stem: Standard radiographic projections for trauma of the facial bones include:
options: Waters (occipitomental), Caldwell (occipitofrontal), and lateral views to evaluate orbital, maxillary, nasal, and zygomatic fractures
rationale: Facial bone series: Waters (best for maxillary sinuses, orbital floors, zygomatic arches), Caldwell (frontal/ethmoid sinuses, orbital margins), lateral (nasal bones, maxillary wall). CT has largely replaced facial bone radiographs for complex trauma evaluation.

stem: Direct digital radiography (DDR) uses which detector type for most clinical applications:
options: Amorphous silicon with cesium iodide scintillator (indirect conversion) providing high DQE and efficient x-ray detection
rationale: Indirect flat panel DR: CsI scintillator (structured phosphor) converts x-rays to light → a-Si photodiode array converts light to electrical signal. Structured CsI reduces light spread → better resolution than unstructured phosphors.

stem: The Bucky factor describes:
options: The increase in patient exposure required when using a grid compared to non-grid technique, due to absorption of primary and scatter radiation by the grid
rationale: Bucky factor: total radiation incident on grid / radiation transmitted through grid. Typical factors: 3-6× (depending on grid ratio). Higher grid ratio = higher Bucky factor = more mAs required = higher patient dose. Trade-off for improved contrast.

stem: An exposure made at 60 kVp and 30 mAs produces adequate density. Using the 15% rule to increase kVp while maintaining density, the new technique would be:
options: 69 kVp at 15 mAs — a 15% increase in kVp allows halving the mAs
rationale: 15% rule: 60 kVp × 1.15 = 69 kVp. Halve the mAs: 30/2 = 15 mAs. New technique: 69 kVp, 15 mAs. Higher kVp increases penetration and reduces contrast (wider latitude). Lower mAs reduces patient dose. Used for dose optimization.

stem: Magnification factor (MF) is calculated as:
options: SID divided by SOD (source-to-object distance), and determines how much larger the image appears compared to the actual object
Grid ratio divided by Bucky factor
Focal spot size divided by SID
rationale: MF = SID/SOD. SOD = SID - OID. If SID = 100 cm and OID = 10 cm: MF = 100/90 = 1.11 (11% magnification). Minimize magnification: decrease OID, increase SID. Intentional magnification: increase OID with small focal spot.

stem: DICOM Worklist allows:
options: Automatic population of patient demographics and exam information from the RIS to the imaging modality, reducing manual entry errors
rationale: DICOM Modality Worklist: scheduled procedures from RIS automatically sent to modality. Technologist selects patient from list → demographics auto-populated in image header. Reduces errors (wrong patient, wrong demographics), improves workflow efficiency.

stem: A charge-coupled device (CCD) in digital imaging:
options: Converts visible light from a phosphor screen into an electrical charge that is read out line by line to create a digital image
Directly converts x-rays to electrical signal
Is used only in CT detectors
Uses magnetic fields to create images
rationale: CCD: silicon chip with photosensitive elements. In radiography: x-rays → phosphor (light) → lens → CCD → digital signal. Used in some DR systems, dental sensors, fluoroscopy cameras. Limited by field coverage and coupling optics.

stem: The characteristic curve of an imaging system shows:
options: The relationship between radiation exposure and image signal/density, revealing the system's speed, latitude, and contrast characteristics
rationale: Characteristic curve (H&D curve for film, response curve for digital): x-axis = log exposure, y-axis = optical density (film) or signal (digital). Film: narrow latitude, defined speed point. Digital: wide latitude, linear response over broad range.

stem: For portable (mobile) chest radiography, the standard SID is:
options: 40-48 inches (102-122 cm), which is shorter than the standard 72-inch PA chest SID and results in greater cardiac magnification
rationale: Portable chest: 40-48 inch SID typical (limited by tube arm length and room constraints). Shorter SID = more magnification = heart appears larger = cannot reliably measure CTR. AP projection further magnifies anterior structures.

stem: Anatomical markers must include:
options: Right (R) or left (L) designation, the technologist's initials, and the date of the examination — placed on the image receptor before exposure
rationale: Anatomical markers: R or L (correct side identification), technologist initials (accountability), date (documentation). Must be placed before exposure — not added digitally afterward. Proper marker placement is a professional responsibility and legal requirement.

stem: The American Registry of Radiologic Technologists (ARRT) certification examination covers which five content areas:
options: Patient Care and Education, Safety, Image Production, Procedures, and Equipment Operation and Quality Control
rationale: ARRT radiography exam: Patient Care (22%), Safety (21%), Image Production (28%), Procedures (27%), Equipment Operation and QC (2%). 200 scored + 20 pilot questions. 3.5 hours. Must pass to use RT(R) credential.
